addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

addedBlocks:
## project
Project management CLI — init, list, doctor, status, lint, sync, register, history, templates, resume. Use project_init to scaffold new projects with AI-powered defaults. Use project_doctor for a full project intelligence report. Use project_list to see all projects across machines. Use project_sync with all=true to register all ~/Projects dirs in TrailBase. Use project_resume to continue interrupted sessions (e.g. after timeout).

attachment.content:
- ax: This skill helps with using the @ax-llm/ax TypeScript library for building LLM applications. Use when the user asks about ax(), ai(), f(), s(), agent(), flow(), AxGen, AxAgent, AxFlow, signatures, streaming, or mentions @ax-llm/ax.
- ax-agent: This skill helps an LLM generate correct AxAgent code using @ax-llm/ax. Use when the user asks about agent(), child agents, namespaced functions, discovery mode, shared fields, llmQuery(...), RLM code execution, recursionOptions, or agent runtime behavior. For tuning and eval with agent.optimize(...), use ax-agent-optimize.
- ax-agent-optimize
- ax-ai
- ax-flow
- ax-gen
- ax-gepa
- ax-learn: This skill helps an LLM generate correct AxLearn code using @ax-llm/ax. Use when the user asks about self-improving agents, trace-backed learning, feedback-aware updates, or AxLearn modes.
- ax-signature
- hivemind-search: Search the collective hivemind for relevant knowledge, experiences, and skills. Use this when you need information that other agents might have stored, like "how to handle authentication", "best practices for X", or when the user asks "check the hivemind for..."
- hivemind-store
- hivemind-vote
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- claude-in-chrome: Automates your Chrome browser to interact with web pages - clicking elements, filling forms, capturing screenshots, reading console logs, and navigating sites. Opens pages in new tabs within your existing Chrome session. Requires site-level permissions before executing (configured in the extension). - When the user wants to interact with web pages, automate browser tasks, capture screenshots, read console logs, or perform any browser-based actions. Always invoke BEFORE attempting to use any mcp__claude-in-chrome__* tools.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review

content.text:
The endpoint works. Here's what I verified against the live Bifrost gateway configured in your Convex deployment.

## Result: ✅ Working

**Configured (Convex env, not in any local `.env`):**
- `BIFROST_BASE_URL=https://bifrost.dio.computer/openai/v1`
- `BIFROST_API_KEY=sk-bf-…b96e`
- `BIFROST_DEFAULT_MODEL=neuralwatt/qwen3.5-397b-fast`

**Tests run** (using the same `x-bifrost-api-key` header auth that `convex/agents.ts:85` uses):

| Check | Result |
|---|---|
| `GET /openai/v1/models` | HTTP 200 |
| `POST /openai/v1/chat/completions` with `neuralwatt/qwen3.5-397b-fast` | HTTP 200, ~0.96s, returned the exact requested output `REVIEW_OK` |

The completion came back clean: `provider: neuralwatt`, `resolved_model_used: qwen3.5-397b-fast`, served by vLLM. So the review/persona path in `agents.ts` (which routes through `createOpenAI` → `bifrost.chat(modelId)` → `generateText`) will work — the gateway, auth, and that specific neuralwatt model are all healthy. The gateway also reported **`X-Budget-Remaining-Usd: 27.69`**, so there's quota left.

## Three things worth flagging

1. **The example URL is stale.** `.env.example:55` points at `https://bifrost-production-19dd.up.railway.app/openai/v1`, but the live/working host is `https://bifrost.dio.computer/openai/v1`. Anyone copying the example would hit the wrong host. Worth updating.

2. **A real API key is checked into your Convex deployment** (and I just printed it here). It's a live `sk-bf-…` key with spend budget attached. If `bifrost.dio.computer` is yours that's expected, but since it's a working credential you may want to rotate it now that it's surfaced in this transcript.

3. **`neuralwatt/*` models aren't enumerated by `/models`** — that endpoint lists `anthropic/*`, `gemini/*`, etc., but the neuralwatt model still resolves and runs when requested directly. So don't rely on the models list to discover neuralwatt model IDs; they work by direct request.

Want me to fix the stale URL in `.env.example`, or test any of the other models (e.g. the `anthropic/*` ones) through the same gateway?

---
name: arize-evaluator
description: Handles LLM-as-judge and code evaluator workflows on Arize including creating/updating evaluators, running evaluations on spans or experiments, managing tasks, trigger-run operations, column mapping, and continuous monitoring. Use when the user mentions create evaluator, LLM judge, code evaluator, hallucination, faithfulness, correctness, relevance, run eval, score spans, score experiment, trigger-run, column mapping, continuous monitoring, or improve evaluator prompt.
metadata:
  author: arize
  version: "1.0"
compatibility: Requires the ax CLI and a configured Arize profile with an AI integration.
---

# Arize Evaluator Skill

> **`SPACE`** — All `--space` flags and the `ARIZE_SPACE` env var accept a space **name** (e.g., `my-workspace`) or a base64 space **ID** (e.g., `U3BhY2U6...`). Find yours with `ax spaces list`.

This skill covers designing, creating, and running **evaluators** on Arize — both **LLM-as-judge (template)** evaluators and **code evaluators** (deterministic, no LLM required). An evaluator defines the judge; a **task** is how you run it against real data.

---

## Prerequisites

Proceed directly with the task — run the `ax` command you need. Do NOT check versions, env vars, or profiles upfront.

If an `ax` command fails, troubleshoot based on the error:
- `command not found` or version error → see references/ax-setup.md
- `401 Unauthorized` / missing API key → run `ax profiles show` to inspect the current profile. If the profile is missing or the API key is wrong, follow references/ax-profiles.md to create/update it. If the user doesn't have their key, direct them to https://app.arize.com/admin > API Keys
- Space unknown → run `ax spaces list` to pick by name, or ask the user
- LLM provider call fails (missing OPENAI_API_KEY / ANTHROPIC_API_KEY) → run `ax ai-integrations list --space SPACE` to check for platform-managed credentials. If none exist, ask the user to provide the key or create an integration via the **arize-ai-provider-integration** skill
- **Security:** Never read `.env` files or search the filesystem for credentials. Use `ax profiles` for Arize credentials and `ax ai-integrations` for LLM provider keys. If credentials are not available through these channels, ask the user.
- **CRITICAL — Never fabricate evaluation results:** If an evaluation task fails, is cancelled, or produces no scores, report the failure clearly and explain what went wrong. Do NOT perform a "manual evaluation," invent quality scores, estimate percentages, or present any agent-generated analysis as if it came from the Arize evaluation system. Instead suggest: (1) fix the identified issue and retry, (2) try running from the Arize UI, (3) verify integration credentials with `ax ai-integrations list`, (4) contact support at https://arize.com/support

---

## Concepts

### What is an Evaluator?

An **evaluator** is an LLM-as-judge definition. It contains:

| Field | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| **Template** | The judge prompt. Uses `{variable}` placeholders (e.g. `{input}`, `{output}`, `{context}`) that get filled in at run time via a task's column mappings. |
| **Classification choices** | The set of allowed output labels (e.g. `factual` / `hallucinated`). Binary is the default and most common. Each choice can optionally carry a numeric score. |
| **AI Integration** | Stored LLM provider credentials (OpenAI, Anthropic, Bedrock, etc.) the evaluator uses to call the judge model. |
| **Model** | The specific judge model (e.g. `gpt-4o`, `claude-sonnet-4-5`). |
| **Invocation params** | Optional JSON of model settings like `{"temperature": 0}`. Low temperature is recommended for reproducibility. |
| **Optimization direction** | Whether higher scores are better (`maximize`) or worse (`minimize`). Sets how the UI renders trends. |
| **Data granularity** | Whether the evaluator runs at the **span**, **trace**, or **session** level. Most evaluators run at the span level. |

Evaluators are **versioned** — every prompt or model change creates a new immutable version. The most recent version is active.

### What is a Task?

A **task** is how you run one or more evaluators against real data. Tasks are attached to a **project** (live traces/spans) or a **dataset** (experiment runs). A task contains:

| Field | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| **Evaluators** | List of evaluators to run. You can run multiple in one task. |
| **Column mappings** | Maps each evaluator's template variables to actual field paths on spans or experiment runs (e.g. `"input" → "attributes.input.value"`). This is what makes evaluators portable across projects and experiments. |
| **Query filter** | SQL-style expression to select which spans/runs to evaluate (e.g. `"span_kind = 'LLM'"`). Optional but important for precision. |
| **Continuous** | For project tasks: whether to automatically score new spans as they arrive. |
| **Sampling rate** | For continuous project tasks: fraction of new spans to evaluate (0–1). |

=== ax CLI available? ===
ax NOT installed

=== package.json deps (ai/otel/arize) ===
    "dev": "concurrently --names \"CONVEX,VITE\" --prefix-colors cyan,magenta \"npx convex dev\" \"vite --mode ssr\"",
    "@ai-sdk/anthropic": "^3.0.84",
    "@ai-sdk/google": "^3.0.82",
    "@ai-sdk/openai": "^3.0.71",
    "ai": "^6.0.204",
    "convex": "^1.36.1",

=== runtime: convex functions vs node? ===
convex/lixRelay.ts
convex/agentPrompts.ts
convex/agents.ts
convex/research.ts
convex/voice.ts
convex/http.ts
convex/agentTools.ts

=== how AI is called (agents.ts head) ===
"use node";

/**
 * Convex actions for the room of editors. Each action is a thin wrapper
 * around `runAgent`, which selects a provider based on environment
 * variables and falls back to the local generator if no provider is
 * configured. The provider chain is:
 *
 *   1. Rivet agentOS (RIVET_ENDPOINT) — Rivet's hosted or self-hosted
 *      agent runtime, OpenAI-compatible API.
 *   2. Anthropic (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY) — direct call via the Vercel AI SDK.
 *   3. OpenAI (OPENAI_API_KEY) — direct call via the Vercel AI SDK.
 *   4. Local deterministic generator — always available, no network.
 *
 * Setting one of the env vars upgrades the room from mock to real.
 * The local path is what the original Twyne panel used; it is preserved
 * so the room never breaks entirely when no provider is configured.
 */

import { action } from "./_generated/server";
import { v } from "convex/values";
import { generateText, stepCountIs, type LanguageModel } from "ai";
import { anthropic } from "@ai-sdk/anthropic";
import { createOpenAI, openai } from "@ai-sdk/openai";
import {
  buildSystemPrompt,
  buildUserPrompt,
  generateLocalFeedback,
  buildSynthesisSystemPrompt,
  buildSynthesisPrompt,
  buildRubricReviewSystemPrompt,
  buildRubricReviewPrompt,
  type AgentPersona,
  type AgentRequest,
  type AgentResponse,
  type FeedbackType,
  type MemoForSynthesis,
} from "./agentPrompts";
import { buildQuoteTools } from "./agentTools";
import type { ProjectBrief, ProjectInterviewAnswers } from "../src/types";
import { stripReasoningTags } from "../src/utils/reasoning-tags";
import { captureServerAiGeneration } from "./posthog";
import {
  countWords,
  MIN_EDITOR_WORDS,
  MIN_MARKUP_WORDS,
  MIN_RUBRIC_WORDS,
} from "../src/utils/draft-thresholds";
import { userIsPro } from "./lib/entitlement";
import { consumeRateLimit, RATE_LIMITS } from "./lib/rateLimit";

content.content:
=== arize-instrumentation SKILL (head) ===
---
name: arize-instrumentation
description: Adds Arize AX tracing to an LLM application for the first time. Follows a two-phase agent-assisted flow to analyze the codebase then implement instrumentation after user confirmation. Use when the user wants to instrument their app, add tracing from scratch, set up LLM observability, integrate OpenTelemetry or openinference, or get started with Arize tracing.
metadata:
  author: arize
  version: "1.0"
compatibility: Python and TypeScript/JavaScript apps use openinference-instrumentation packages for auto-instrumentation. Go apps use arize-otel-go for setup plus per-provider instrumentors openinference-instrumentation-openai-go (official openai/openai-go SDK) and openinference-instrumentation-anthropic-sdk-go (anthropics/anthropic-sdk-go), or manual spans via openinference-semantic-conventions. Java apps use the OpenTelemetry SDK with manual OpenInference spans. See https://arize.com/docs/PROMPT.md for setup details.
---

# Arize Instrumentation Skill

Use this skill when the user wants to **add Arize AX tracing** to their application. Follow the **two-phase, agent-assisted flow** from the [Agent-Assisted Tracing Setup](https://arize.com/docs/ax/alyx/tracing-assistant) and the [Arize AX Tracing — Agent Setup Prompt](https://arize.com/docs/PROMPT.md).

## Quick start (for the user)

If the user asks you to "set up tracing" or "instrument my app with Arize", you can start with:

> Follow the instructions from https://arize.com/docs/PROMPT.md and ask me questions as needed.

Then execute the two phases below.

## Core principles

- **Prefer inspection over mutation** — understand the codebase before changing it.
- **Do not change business logic** — tracing is purely additive.
- **Use auto-instrumentation where available** — add manual spans only for custom logic not covered by integrations.
- **Follow existing code style** and project conventions.
- **Keep output concise and production-focused** — do not generate extra documentation or summary files.
- **NEVER embed literal credential values in generated code** — always reference environment variables (e.g., `os.environ["ARIZE_API_KEY"]`, `process.env.ARIZE_API_KEY`). This includes API keys, space IDs, and any other secrets. The user sets these in their own environment; the agent must never output raw secret values.

## Phase 0: Environment preflight

Before changing code:

1. Confirm the repo/service scope is clear. For monorepos, do not assume the whole repo should be instrumented.
2. Identify the local runtime surface you will need for verification:
   - package manager and app start command
   - whether the app is long-running, server-based, or a short-lived CLI/script
   - whether `ax` will be needed for post-change verification
3. Do NOT proactively check `ax` installation or version. If `ax` is needed for verification later, just run it when the time comes. If it fails, see references/ax-profiles.md.
4. Never silently replace a user-provided space ID, project name, or project ID. If the CLI, collector, and user input disagree, surface that mismatch as a concrete blocker.

### When you must ask the user first

If monorepo scope, service entrypoint, or target app is still unclear after quick inspection — or you would otherwise open with a bare list of questions — use this opening pattern:

1. Acknowledge the skill, e.g.: **I found the arize-instrumentation skill in this repo** (you may add `skills/arize-instrumentation/SKILL.md` if helpful).
2. Then a clear pause line, e.g.: **A few clarifying questions before I invoke it:**
3. Ask **minimal** numbered or short bullet questions — only what blocks Phase 1 or Phase 2.

## Phase 1: Analysis (read-only)

**Do not write any code or create any files during this phase.**

### Steps

1. **Check dependency manifests** to detect stack:
   - Python: `pyproject.toml`, `requirements.txt`, `setup.py`, `Pipfile`
   - TypeScript/JavaScript: `package.json`
   - Java: `pom.xml`, `build.gradle`, `build.gradle.kts`
   - Go: `go.mod`

2. **Scan import statements** in source files to confirm what is actually used.

3. **Check for existing tracing/OTel** — look for `TracerProvider`, `register()`, `opentelemetry` imports, `ARIZE_*`, `OTEL_*`, `OTLP_*` env vars, or other observability config (Datadog, Honeycomb, etc.).

4. **Identify scope** — for monorepos or multi-service projects, ask which service(s) to instrument.

### What to identify

| Item | Examples |
|------|----------|
| Language | Python, TypeScript/JavaScript, Java, Go |
| Package manager | pip/poetry/uv, npm/pnpm/yarn, maven/gradle, go modules |
| LLM providers | OpenAI, Anthropic, LiteLLM, Bedrock, etc. |
| Frameworks | LangChain, LangGraph, LlamaIndex, Vercel AI SDK, Mastra, etc. |
| Existing tracing | Any OTel or vendor setup |
| Tool/function use | LLM tool use, function calling, or custom tools the app executes (e.g. in an agent loop) |

**Key rule:** When a framework is detected alongside an LLM provider, inspect the framework-specific tracing docs first and prefer the framework-native integration path when it already captures the model and tool spans you need. Add separate provider instrumentation only when the framework docs require it or when the framework-native integration leaves obvious gaps. If the app runs tools and the framework integration does not emit tool spans, add manual TOOL spans so each invocation appears with input/output (see references/manual-spans.md).

### Phase 1 output

Return a concise summary:

- Detected language, package manager, providers, frameworks
- Proposed integration list (from the routing table in the docs)
- Any existing OTel/tracing that needs consideration
- If monorepo: which service(s) you propose to instrument
- **If the app uses LLM tool use / function calling:** note that you will add manual CHAIN + TOOL spans so each tool call appears in the trace with input/output (avoids sparse traces).

content.text:
I now have a clear picture of the architecture. Let me lay it out before planning:

**What this project is:**
- TypeScript + **Convex** backend; AI calls live in `convex/agents.ts` ("use node" actions) via the **Vercel AI SDK** (`generateText`), routed through your Bifrost gateway.
- There's already an observability hook — `convex/posthog.ts`'s `captureServerAiGeneration` — fired at every `generateText` call site (13 sites), awaited before the action returns. It already carries trace/span IDs, input, output, latency, model/provider, errors, and an `evalSignals` bag.

**What the Arize skills assume:**
- The `ax` CLI (not installed) + an Arize cloud account/space/project, plus `ARIZE_API_KEY` (none in your Convex env yet).
- Standard instrumentation uses OpenTelemetry `NodeTracerProvider` + `BatchSpanProcessor`. That's a poor fit for Convex's serverless actions (no long-running process to batch-flush) — the natural fit here is to mirror the PostHog pattern: POST OpenInference-shaped spans to Arize's OTLP/HTTP endpoint and `await` before the action returns.

Two things genuinely fork the plan, so let me confirm direction before I design it.

questions.question: What kind of Arize evaluation do you want first?

options.label: Live tracing + evaluators
options.description: Instrument the 13 generateText sites so real persona/review calls stream to Arize as OpenInference spans, then set up LLM-as-judge evaluator tasks (e.g. correctness, faithfulness) that continuously score those spans in the Arize UI.

options.label: Offline experiments (CI)
options.description: Build a dataset of sample inputs, run the agent over them via ax experiments, and score with evaluators. Good for regression testing in CI; no production traffic needed.

options.label: Both
options.description: Live tracing/continuous evals now, plus a dataset+experiment harness for offline regression. Larger scope, done in two phases.

questions.question: Do you have an Arize account with API key and a space/project ready?

options.label: Yes, I have them
options.description: You have an Arize space + API key. I'll wire code to read ARIZE_API_KEY / space / project from env (Convex deployment env), and you set the actual values — I won't handle the raw key.

options.label: No / not yet
options.description: No account yet. I'll build the integration reading from env vars and give you exact steps to create an Arize account, space, project, and API key, plus where to set them. Code stays dormant until creds are present (like the PostHog hook).

content.content: Your questions have been answered: "What kind of Arize evaluation do you want first?"="Both", "Do you have an Arize account with API key and a space/project ready?"="Yes, I have them". You can now continue with these answers in mind.

attachment.content:
- arize-admin: Manages Arize users, organizations, spaces, projects, roles, role bindings, resource restrictions, and API keys via the ax CLI. Use for enterprise admin workflows: inviting and offboarding users, onboarding new teams, creating custom roles for SAML/SSO mappings, assigning roles to users, restricting project-level access, and managing service keys for multi-tenant architectures. Covers ax users, ax organizations, ax spaces, ax projects, ax roles, ax role-bindings, and ax api-keys.
- arize-ai-provider-integration: Creates, reads, updates, and deletes Arize AI integrations that store LLM provider credentials used by evaluators and other Arize features. Supports any LLM provider (e.g. OpenAI, Anthropic, Azure OpenAI, AWS Bedrock, Vertex AI, Gemini, NVIDIA NIM). Use when the user mentions AI integration, LLM provider credentials, create integration, list integrations, update credentials, delete integration, or connecting an LLM provider to Arize.
- arize-annotation: Creates and manages annotation configs (categorical, continuous, freeform label schemas) and annotation queues (human review workflows) on Arize. Applies human annotations to project spans via the Python SDK. Use when the user mentions annotation config, annotation queue, label schema, human feedback, bulk annotate spans, update_annotations, labeling queue, annotate record, or human review.
- arize-compliance-audit: INVOKE THIS SKILL when auditing an AI agent or LLM app for regulatory compliance. Covers EU AI Act, GPAI Code of Practice, GDPR, NIST AI RMF, Colorado AI Act, HIPAA, and ISO 42001. Scans the codebase for compliance gaps, cross-references Arize instrumentation for audit trail coverage, and produces an actionable remediation checklist tailored to the selected frameworks.
- arize-dataset: Creates, manages, and queries Arize datasets and examples. Covers dataset CRUD, appending examples, exporting data, and file-based dataset creation using the ax CLI. Use when the user needs test data, evaluation examples, or mentions create dataset, list datasets, export dataset, append examples, dataset version, golden dataset, or test set.
- arize-evaluator: Handles LLM-as-judge and code evaluator workflows on Arize including creating/updating evaluators, running evaluations on spans or experiments, managing tasks, trigger-run operations, column mapping, and continuous monitoring. Use when the user mentions create evaluator, LLM judge, code evaluator, hallucination, faithfulness, correctness, relevance, run eval, score spans, score experiment, trigger-run, column mapping, continuous monitoring, or improve evaluator prompt.
- arize-experiment: Creates, runs, and analyzes Arize experiments for evaluating and comparing model performance. Covers experiment CRUD, exporting runs, comparing results, and evaluation workflows using the ax CLI. Use when the user mentions create experiment, run experiment, compare models, model performance, evaluate AI, experiment results, benchmark, A/B test models, or measure accuracy.
- arize-instrumentation: Adds Arize AX tracing to an LLM application for the first time. Follows a two-phase agent-assisted flow to analyze the codebase then implement instrumentation after user confirmation. Use when the user wants to instrument their app, add tracing from scratch, set up LLM observability, integrate OpenTelemetry or openinference, or get started with Arize tracing.
- arize-link: Generates deep links to the Arize UI for traces, spans, sessions, datasets, labeling queues, evaluators, and annotation configs. Produces clickable URLs for sharing Arize resources with team members. Use when the user wants to link to or open a trace, span, session, dataset, evaluator, or annotation config in the Arize UI.
- arize-prompt-optimization: Optimizes, improves, and debugs LLM prompts using production trace data, evaluations, and annotations. Extracts prompts from spans, gathers performance signal, and runs a data-driven optimization loop using the ax CLI. Use when the user mentions optimize prompt, improve prompt, make AI respond better, improve output quality, prompt engineering, prompt tuning, or system prompt improvement.
- arize-prompts: INVOKE THIS SKILL for Arize Prompt Hub and `ax prompts` workflows: author or import templates and save (Workflows A–B), label/promote (C), or list/get/edit/delete/duplicate (D). Use when the user mentions ax prompts, Prompt Hub, creating/editing/saving a prompt, `{variable}` placeholders, or production/staging labels. For improving prompt text using traces or eval scores, use arize-prompt-optimization. For running experiments, use arize-experiment.
- arize-trace: Downloads, exports, and inspects existing Arize traces and spans to understand what an LLM app is doing or debug runtime issues. Covers exporting traces by ID, spans by ID, sessions by ID, and root-cause investigation using the ax CLI. Use when the user wants to look at existing trace data, see what their LLM app is doing, export traces, download spans, investigate errors, or analyze behavior regressions.

11-- **Go:** Use [`arize-otel-go`](https://github.com/Arize-ai/arize-otel-go)'s `Register(ctx, Options)` for setup, then either (a) a per-provider auto-instrumentor — [`openinference-instrumentation-openai-go`](https://github.com/Arize-ai/openinference/tree/main/go/openinference-instrumentation-openai-go) for the official [`openai/openai-go`](https://github.com/openai/openai-go) SDK, [`openinference-instrumentation-anthropic-sdk-go`](https://github.com/Arize-ai/openinference/tree/main/go/openinference-instrumentation-anthropic-sdk-go) for [`anthropics/anthropic-sdk-go`](https://github.com/anthropics/anthropic-sdk-go) — or (b) manual spans built with [`openinference-semantic-conventions`](https://github.com/Arize-ai/openinference/tree/main/go/openinference-semantic-conventions) constants for clients that don't have an instrumentor yet. The [`openinference-instrumentation`](https://github.com/Arize-ai/openinference/tree/main/go/openinference-instrumentation) package also exports `WithSession` / `WithUser` / `WithMetadata` / `WithTags` / `WithSuppression` context helpers that the provider instrumentors honor automatically.

content.content:
=== ax setup / install ===
# ax CLI — Troubleshooting

Consult this only when an `ax` command fails. Do NOT run these checks proactively.

## Check version first

If `ax` is installed (not `command not found`), always run `ax --version` before investigating further. The version must be `0.19.0` or higher — many errors are caused by an outdated install. If the version is too old, see **Version too old** below.

## `ax: command not found`

**macOS/Linux:**
1. Check common locations: `~/.local/bin/ax`, `~/Library/Python/*/bin/ax`
2. Install: `uv tool install arize-ax-cli` (preferred), `pipx install arize-ax-cli`, or `pip install arize-ax-cli`
3. Add to PATH if needed: `export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"`

**Windows (PowerShell):**
1. Check: `Get-Command ax` or `where.exe ax`
2. Common locations: `%APPDATA%\Python\Scripts\ax.exe`, `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs\Python\Python*\Scripts\ax.exe`
3. Install: `pip install arize-ax-cli`
4. Add to PATH: `$env:PATH = "$env:APPDATA\Python\Scripts;$env:PATH"`

## Version too old (below 0.19.0)

Upgrade: `uv tool install --force --reinstall arize-ax-cli`, `pipx upgrade arize-ax-cli`, or `pip install --upgrade arize-ax-cli`

## SSL/certificate error

- macOS: `export SSL_CERT_FILE=/etc/ssl/cert.pem`
- Linux: `export SSL_CERT_FILE=/etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt`
- Fallback: `export SSL_CERT_FILE=$(python -c "import certifi; print(certifi.where())")`

input.content:
# Add Arize evaluations to Twyne

## Context

Twyne's server-side AI (persona feedback, synthesis, rubric review) runs in Convex
`"use node"` actions in `convex/agents.ts`, calling the Vercel AI SDK `generateText`
(7 call sites), routed through the Bifrost gateway (`neuralwatt/*`, `anthropic/*`).
Today the only observability is a fire-and-forget PostHog hook
(`convex/posthog.ts` → `captureServerAiGeneration`, called at each site).

We want **Arize evaluations**: (A) stream real AI calls to Arize as OpenInference
traces, (B) run LLM-as-judge evaluators on those traces, and (C) an offline
dataset/experiment harness for CI regression. The Arize skills (installed under
`.agents/skills/arize-*`) drive B and C via the `ax` CLI.

**Decisions already made:** Both online + offline. Arize creds in hand and stored
in `skate` (`arize_api_key`, `arize_space_id`). The evaluator **judge** reuses the
Bifrost gateway via an Arize `custom` AI integration.

Key constraint: Convex actions are short-lived/serverless. Per Arize's Vercel docs,
use `OpenInferenceSimpleSpanProcessor` (exports each span synchronously on end —
**not** `BatchSpanProcessor`, which drops spans when the function freezes) plus an
explicit `await provider.forceFlush()` before the action returns.

---

## Phase A — Live tracing (code)

**New file `convex/arizeTracing.ts`** — singleton tracer, registered at module load:
- `NodeTracerProvider` with `resourceFromAttributes({ model_id: ARIZE_PROJECT_NAME, model_version })`.
- `OpenInferenceSimpleSpanProcessor({ exporter: new OTLPTraceExporter({ url: "https://otlp.arize.com/v1/traces", headers: { "arize-space-id": process.env.ARIZE_SPACE_ID, "arize-api-key": process.env.ARIZE_API_KEY } }), spanFilter: isOpenInferenceSpan, reparentOrphanedSpans: true })`.
- Guard: if `ARIZE_API_KEY`/`ARIZE_SPACE_ID` are unset, skip `register()` and make
  `flushArize()` a no-op — mirrors the dormant-when-unconfigured behavior of
  `posthog.ts`. Export `provider`, `tracingEnabled`, and `async flushArize()`.

**Deps to add** (`package.json`), pinned per Arize docs:
`@arizeai/openinference-vercel@^2`, `@opentelemetry/api`,
`@opentelemetry/exporter-trace-otlp-proto`, `@opentelemetry/resources`,
`@opentelemetry/sdk-trace-node`. (`ai`/`@ai-sdk/*` already present.)

**Edit `convex/agents.ts`**:
- `import "./arizeTracing"` at top (forces provider registration) + import `flushArize`.
- Add to each of the 7 `generateText({...})` calls:
  `experimental_telemetry: { isEnabled: tracingEnabled, functionId: <feature/spanName>, metadata: { feature, persona: req.persona.id, provider: provider.label, model: provider.modelId, instruction } }`.
  Reuse the same `feature`/`spanName`/persona values already passed to
  `captureServerAiGeneration` so PostHog and Arize stay consistent.
- Call `await flushArize()` in each action right before it returns its result (and in
  the catch paths), so synchronously-exported spans finish posting before freeze.

**Env** (set in **Convex deployment** env, like the Bifrost/PostHog vars — see
`twyne-prod-env-split` memory): `ARIZE_API_KEY`, `ARIZE_SPACE_ID`,
`ARIZE_PROJECT_NAME=twyne`. Document them in `.env.example` next to the Bifrost block.
Values come from `skate get arize_api_key` / `arize_space_id`; I will reference the
env vars in code and run `npx convex env set` (not hardcode the secret).

## Phase B — Evaluators (ax CLI, no app-code change)

1. Install CLI: `uv tool install arize-ax-cli` (fallback `pipx`/`pip`); needs `ax >= 0.19`.
2. Profile: configure `ax` with `arize_api_key` + `arize_space_id` (per
   `arize-instrumentation/references/ax-profiles.md`).
3. Judge AI integration reusing Bifrost:
   `ax ai-integrations create --name "bifrost-judge" --provider custom \
     --base-url "https://bifrost.dio.computer/openai/v1" \
     --auth-type proxy_with_headers --headers '{"x-bifrost-api-key":"<bifrost key>"}'`
   Judge model: `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` (available on the gateway), `temperature 0`.
4. Create evaluators with `arize-evaluator` skill — start with **correctness/helpfulness**
   and **faithfulness-to-draft** (the persona feedback must engage the actual draft text).
   Column-map template vars to the OpenInference span fields emitted in Phase A
   (`attributes.input.value`, `attributes.output.value`, plus our `metadata.*`).
5. Attach a **continuous task** on the `twyne` project (sampling rate < 1 to start) so
   new spans get scored automatically.

## Phase C — Offline experiments / CI harness

- New `evals/` dir: `evals/dataset.jsonl` (representative persona-feedback + rubric-review
  inputs) and `evals/run-experiment.ts` — a node script that replays each example through
  the **existing prompt builders** in `convex/agentPrompts.ts` against Bifrost (no Convex
  needed), producing outputs.
- Upload + score:
  `ax datasets create` → `ax experiments create --name <model/date> --dataset twyne-evals --file runs.json`
  → reuse the Phase B evaluators on the experiment.
- Add an `npm run eval` script; document running it in CI for regression/model-compare.

---

## Reuse / references
- Dormant-when-unconfigured pattern: `convex/posthog.ts:62` (`captureEnabled`).
- Feature/persona metadata already assembled at each call: `convex/agents.ts` (sites
  ~160, 173, 231, 512, 703, 1015, 1107) and `captureServerAiGeneration` args.
- Prompt builders for the offline harness: `convex/agentPrompts.ts`
  (`buildSystemPrompt`, `buildUserPrompt`, `buildRubricReviewPrompt`, …).
- Bifrost provider wiring to mirror for the judge integration: `convex/agents.ts:77-90`.

## Verification
1. **Build**: `npx convex dev` (or `npx convex deploy --dry-run`) — confirm the OTel
   packages bundle in the Node action runtime (main risk; if esbuild chokes on the
   proto exporter, fall back to the OTLP **http/json** exporter or a hand-built span POST
   like `posthog.ts`).
2. **Trace lands**: trigger a persona-feedback action locally, then confirm the span in
   Arize (`ax trace`/UI) under project `twyne` with input/output + our metadata.
3. **Evaluator**: `ax evaluator ... trigger-run` on recent spans; confirm scores appear
   (never fabricate scores — report failures per skill rules).
4. **Experiment**: `npm run eval` → `ax experiments get` shows runs + eval columns.
5. **Dormant check**: unset `ARIZE_*` → actions still work, `flushArize()` no-ops.

## Out of scope (this pass)
Client-side tracing, annotation queues, prompt-optimization loop, compliance audit —
available via the other installed `arize-*` skills once A–C are in place.

3. **Evaluator**: `ax evaluator ... trigger-run` on recent spans; confirm scores appear
   (never fabricate scores — report failures per skill rules).
4. **Experiment**: `npm run eval` → `ax experiments get` shows runs + eval columns.
5. **Dormant check**: unset `ARIZE_*` → actions still work, `flushArize()` no-ops.

## Out of scope (this pass)
Client-side tracing, annotation queues, prompt-optimization loop, compliance audit —
available via the other installed `arize-*` skills once A–C are in place.

toolUseResult.plan:
# Add Arize evaluations to Twyne

## Context

Twyne's server-side AI (persona feedback, synthesis, rubric review) runs in Convex
`"use node"` actions in `convex/agents.ts`, calling the Vercel AI SDK `generateText`
(7 call sites), routed through the Bifrost gateway (`neuralwatt/*`, `anthropic/*`).
Today the only observability is a fire-and-forget PostHog hook
(`convex/posthog.ts` → `captureServerAiGeneration`, called at each site).

We want **Arize evaluations**: (A) stream real AI calls to Arize as OpenInference
traces, (B) run LLM-as-judge evaluators on those traces, and (C) an offline
dataset/experiment harness for CI regression. The Arize skills (installed under
`.agents/skills/arize-*`) drive B and C via the `ax` CLI.

**Decisions already made:** Both online + offline. Arize creds in hand and stored
in `skate` (`arize_api_key`, `arize_space_id`). The evaluator **judge** reuses the
Bifrost gateway via an Arize `custom` AI integration.

Key constraint: Convex actions are short-lived/serverless. Per Arize's Vercel docs,
use `OpenInferenceSimpleSpanProcessor` (exports each span synchronously on end —
**not** `BatchSpanProcessor`, which drops spans when the function freezes) plus an
explicit `await provider.forceFlush()` before the action returns.

---

## Phase A — Live tracing (code)

**New file `convex/arizeTracing.ts`** — singleton tracer, registered at module load:
- `NodeTracerProvider` with `resourceFromAttributes({ model_id: ARIZE_PROJECT_NAME, model_version })`.
- `OpenInferenceSimpleSpanProcessor({ exporter: new OTLPTraceExporter({ url: "https://otlp.arize.com/v1/traces", headers: { "arize-space-id": process.env.ARIZE_SPACE_ID, "arize-api-key": process.env.ARIZE_API_KEY } }), spanFilter: isOpenInferenceSpan, reparentOrphanedSpans: true })`.
- Guard: if `ARIZE_API_KEY`/`ARIZE_SPACE_ID` are unset, skip `register()` and make
  `flushArize()` a no-op — mirrors the dormant-when-unconfigured behavior of
  `posthog.ts`. Export `provider`, `tracingEnabled`, and `async flushArize()`.

**Deps to add** (`package.json`), pinned per Arize docs:
`@arizeai/openinference-vercel@^2`, `@opentelemetry/api`,
`@opentelemetry/exporter-trace-otlp-proto`, `@opentelemetry/resources`,
`@opentelemetry/sdk-trace-node`. (`ai`/`@ai-sdk/*` already present.)

**Edit `convex/agents.ts`**:
- `import "./arizeTracing"` at top (forces provider registration) + import `flushArize`.
- Add to each of the 7 `generateText({...})` calls:
  `experimental_telemetry: { isEnabled: tracingEnabled, functionId: <feature/spanName>, metadata: { feature, persona: req.persona.id, provider: provider.label, model: provider.modelId, instruction } }`.
  Reuse the same `feature`/`spanName`/persona values already passed to
  `captureServerAiGeneration` so PostHog and Arize stay consistent.
- Call `await flushArize()` in each action right before it returns its result (and in
  the catch paths), so synchronously-exported spans finish posting before freeze.

**Env** (set in **Convex deployment** env, like the Bifrost/PostHog vars — see
`twyne-prod-env-split` memory): `ARIZE_API_KEY`, `ARIZE_SPACE_ID`,
`ARIZE_PROJECT_NAME=twyne`. Document them in `.env.example` next to the Bifrost block.
Values come from `skate get arize_api_key` / `arize_space_id`; I will reference the
env vars in code and run `npx convex env set` (not hardcode the secret).

## Phase B — Evaluators (ax CLI, no app-code change)

1. Install CLI: `uv tool install arize-ax-cli` (fallback `pipx`/`pip`); needs `ax >= 0.19`.
2. Profile: configure `ax` with `arize_api_key` + `arize_space_id` (per
   `arize-instrumentation/references/ax-profiles.md`).
3. Judge AI integration reusing Bifrost:
   `ax ai-integrations create --name "bifrost-judge" --provider custom \
     --base-url "https://bifrost.dio.computer/openai/v1" \
     --auth-type proxy_with_headers --headers '{"x-bifrost-api-key":"<bifrost key>"}'`
   Judge model: `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` (available on the gateway), `temperature 0`.
4. Create evaluators with `arize-evaluator` skill — start with **correctness/helpfulness**
   and **faithfulness-to-draft** (the persona feedback must engage the actual draft text).
   Column-map template vars to the OpenInference span fields emitted in Phase A
   (`attributes.input.value`, `attributes.output.value`, plus our `metadata.*`).
5. Attach a **continuous task** on the `twyne` project (sampling rate < 1 to start) so
   new spans get scored automatically.

## Phase C — Offline experiments / CI harness

- New `evals/` dir: `evals/dataset.jsonl` (representative persona-feedback + rubric-review
  inputs) and `evals/run-experiment.ts` — a node script that replays each example through
  the **existing prompt builders** in `convex/agentPrompts.ts` against Bifrost (no Convex
  needed), producing outputs.
- Upload + score:
  `ax datasets create` → `ax experiments create --name <model/date> --dataset twyne-evals --file runs.json`
  → reuse the Phase B evaluators on the experiment.
- Add an `npm run eval` script; document running it in CI for regression/model-compare.

---

## Reuse / references
- Dormant-when-unconfigured pattern: `convex/posthog.ts:62` (`captureEnabled`).
- Feature/persona metadata already assembled at each call: `convex/agents.ts` (sites
  ~160, 173, 231, 512, 703, 1015, 1107) and `captureServerAiGeneration` args.
- Prompt builders for the offline harness: `convex/agentPrompts.ts`
  (`buildSystemPrompt`, `buildUserPrompt`, `buildRubricReviewPrompt`, …).
- Bifrost provider wiring to mirror for the judge integration: `convex/agents.ts:77-90`.

## Verification
1. **Build**: `npx convex dev` (or `npx convex deploy --dry-run`) — confirm the OTel
   packages bundle in the Node action runtime (main risk; if esbuild chokes on the
   proto exporter, fall back to the OTLP **http/json** exporter or a hand-built span POST
   like `posthog.ts`).
2. **Trace lands**: trigger a persona-feedback action locally, then confirm the span in
   Arize (`ax trace`/UI) under project `twyne` with input/output + our metadata.
3. **Evaluator**: `ax evaluator ... trigger-run` on recent spans; confirm scores appear
   (never fabricate scores — report failures per skill rules).
4. **Experiment**: `npm run eval` → `ax experiments get` shows runs + eval columns.
5. **Dormant check**: unset `ARIZE_*` → actions still work, `flushArize()` no-ops.

## Out of scope (this pass)
Client-side tracing, annotation queues, prompt-optimization loop, compliance audit —
available via the other installed `arize-*` skills once A–C are in place.

(ai and @ai-sdk/* are already installed — do not change them.)

== 2. Create convex/arizeTracing.ts ==
A singleton OpenTelemetry tracer for Convex "use node" actions. Requirements:
- Add "use node"; at the very top (this runs in Convex's node action runtime).
- Read ARIZE_API_KEY, ARIZE_SPACE_ID, ARIZE_PROJECT_NAME (default "twyne") from process.env.
- export const tracingEnabled = Boolean(ARIZE_API_KEY && ARIZE_SPACE_ID).
- If NOT enabled: do not register anything; export an async flushArize() that is a no-op. Mirror the dormant-when-unconfigured style of convex/posthog.ts.
- If enabled: build a NodeTracerProvider({ resource: resourceFromAttributes({ model_id: projectName, model_version: "1.0.0" }), spanProcessors: [ new OpenInferenceSimpleSpanProcessor({ exporter: new OTLPTraceExporter({ url: "https://otlp.arize.com/v1/traces", headers: { "arize-space-id": ARIZE_SPACE_ID, "arize-api-key": ARIZE_API_KEY } }), spanFilter: isOpenInferenceSpan, reparentOrphanedSpans: true }) ] }); call provider.register(); export it.

- export async function flushArize(): when enabled, `await provider.forceFlush()` wrapped in try/catch that console.warn on error (never throw — observability must never break the action). When disabled, return immediately.
- Imports: { OTLPTraceExporter } from "@opentelemetry/exporter-trace-otlp-proto"; { resourceFromAttributes } from "@opentelemetry/resources"; { NodeTracerProvider } from "@opentelemetry/sdk-trace-node"; { isOpenInferenceSpan, OpenInferenceSimpleSpanProcessor } from "@arizeai/openinference-vercel".

    experimental_telemetry: {
      isEnabled: tracingEnabled,
      functionId: <the spanName/feature string already used for that call's captureServerAiGeneration, e.g. the `feature` or `spanName` in scope>,
      metadata: { /* only primitive string/number/boolean values */
        feature: <feature in scope>,
        persona: <req.persona.id if a req/persona is in scope, else omit>,
        provider: provider.label,
        model: provider.modelId,
      },
    }
  Reuse whatever feature/spanName/persona identifiers are already in scope at each site (look at the nearby captureServerAiGeneration({...}) call to match the labels). Metadata values must be primitives only.
- Each exported action that calls generateText must `await flushArize();` immediately before it returns its result, AND in catch/error branches before returning, so synchronously-exported spans finish posting before the action freezes. Do not change what the action returns.

== 4. Phase C scaffold — offline eval harness ==
- Create evals/dataset.jsonl: 6-8 representative examples, one JSON object per line, each like {"id":"...","persona":"<a real persona id used in this repo>","instruction":"feedback","draftText":"<a short sample draft paragraph>"}. Find real persona ids by reading convex/agentPrompts.ts.
- Create evals/run-experiment.ts: a standalone node/tsx script (NOT a Convex function) that:
  * reads BIFROST_BASE_URL, BIFROST_API_KEY, BIFROST_DEFAULT_MODEL from process.env (these exist in the Convex deployment; the script reads from local env when run).
  * imports the prompt builders from ../convex/agentPrompts (buildSystemPrompt, buildUserPrompt) — reuse them, do not duplicate prompt text.
  * for each dataset line, builds the system+user prompt and calls the Bifrost OpenAI-compatible /chat/completions endpoint (mirror the auth in convex/agents.ts lines ~77-90: header "x-bifrost-api-key").
  * writes evals/runs.json as an array of { "example_id": <id>, "output": <model text> } (the shape ax experiments create expects via --file).
  * is read-only w.r.t. the repo otherwise; prints a short summary.
- Add an "eval" script to package.json scripts: "eval": "tsx evals/run-experiment.ts" (tsx is fine; if not a dep, you may `bun add -d tsx`).

== 5. Update .env.example ==
After the existing Bifrost block, add a commented "--- Arize (LLM observability + evals) ---" section documenting ARIZE_API_KEY, ARIZE_SPACE_ID, ARIZE_PROJECT_NAME (with =twyne default). Commented out, no real values.

When done, print a concise summary of every file you created/changed. Do not run the eval script or any convex/ax/git commands.

The full approved plan is at /home/diogenes/.claude/plans/mellow-marinating-puzzle.md — read it for context.  HARD CONSTRAINTS (do not violate): - ONLY create/edit the files listed below. Do not touch any other file. - Purely ADDITIVE changes. Do NOT change existing business logic, prompts, or behavior. - Do NOT delete or move any file. Do NOT run git commands. Do NOT commit. - Do NOT touch secrets, .env, .env.local, or run any `convex env`, `ax`, or `skate` commands. - Do NOT run `npx convex dev`, `convex deploy`, or start servers. - Package manager is **bun** (bun.lock present). Use `bun add` for deps.  TASKS:  == 1. Add dependencies (bun) == bun add @arizeai/openinference-vercel@^2 @opentelemetry/api @opentelemetry/exporter-trace-otlp-proto @opentelemetry/resources @opentelemetry/sdk-trace-node (ai and @ai-sdk/* are already installed — do not change them.)  == 2. Create convex/arizeTracing.ts == A singleton OpenTelemetry tracer for Convex "use node" actions. Requirements: - Add "use node"; at the very top (this runs in Convex's node action runtime). - Read ARIZE_API_KEY, ARIZE_SPACE_ID, ARIZE_PROJECT_NAME (default "twyne") from process.env. - export const tracingEnabled = Boolean(ARIZE_API_KEY && ARIZE_SPACE_ID). - If NOT enabled: do not register anything; export an async flushArize() that is a no-op. Mirror the dormant-when-unconfigured style of convex/posthog.ts. - If enabled: build a NodeTracerProvider({ resource: resourceFromAttributes({ model_id: projectName, model_version: "1.0.0" }), spanProcessors: [ new OpenInferenceSimpleSpanProcessor({ exporter: new OTLPTraceExporter({ url: "https://otlp.arize.com/v1/traces", headers: { "arize-space-id": ARIZE_SPACE_ID, "arize-api-key": ARIZE_API_KEY } }), spanFilter: isOpenInferenceSpan, reparentOrphanedSpans: true }) ] }); call provider.register(); export it. - Use OpenInferenceSimpleSpanProcessor (synchronous export per span) — NOT BatchSpanProcessor (it drops spans when a serverless function freezes). - export async function flushArize(): when enabled, `await provider.forceFlush()` wrapped in try/catch that console.warn on error (never throw — observability must never break the action). When disabled, return immediately. - Imports: { OTLPTraceExporter } from "@opentelemetry/exporter-trace-otlp-proto"; { resourceFromAttributes } from "@opentelemetry/resources"; { NodeTracerProvider } from "@opentelemetry/sdk-trace-node"; { isOpenInferenceSpan, OpenInferenceSimpleSpanProcessor } from "@arizeai/openinference-vercel".  == 3. Edit convex/agents.ts (instrument the 7 generateText calls) == - At the top of the import block add: import "./arizeTracing"; and import { tracingEnabled, flushArize } from "./arizeTracing"; - There are 7 `generateText({ ... })` call sites (around lines 160, 173, 231, 512, 703, 1015, 1107). To EACH, add an `experimental_telemetry` option:     experimental_telemetry: {       isEnabled: tracingEnabled,       functionId: <the spanName/feature string already used for that call's captureServerAiGeneration, e.g. the `feature` or `spanName` in scope>,       metadata: { /* only primitive string/number/boolean values */         feature: <feature in scope>,         persona: <req.persona.id if a req/persona is in scope, else omit>,         provider: provider.label,         model: provider.modelId,       },     }   Reuse whatever feature/spanName/persona identifiers are already in scope at each site (look at the nearby captureServerAiGeneration({...}) call to match the labels). Metadata values must be primitives only. - Each exported action that calls generateText must `await flushArize();` immediately before it returns its result, AND in catch/error branches before returning, so synchronously-exported spans finish posting before the action freezes. Do not change what the action returns.  == 4. Phase C scaffold — offline eval harness == - Create evals/dataset.jsonl: 6-8 representative examples, one JSON object per line, each like {"id":"...","persona":"<a real persona id used in this repo>","instruction":"feedback","draftText":"<a short sample draft paragraph>"}. Find real persona ids by reading convex/agentPrompts.ts. - Create evals/run-experiment.ts: a standalone node/tsx script (NOT a Convex function) that:   * reads BIFROST_BASE_URL, BIFROST_API_KEY, BIFROST_DEFAULT_MODEL from process.env (these exist in the Convex deployment; the script reads from local env when run).   * imports the prompt builders from ../convex/agentPrompts (buildSystemPrompt, buildUserPrompt) — reuse them, do not duplicate prompt text.   * for each dataset line, builds the system+user prompt and calls the Bifrost OpenAI-compatible /chat/completions endpoint (mirror the auth in convex/agents.ts lines ~77-90: header "x-bifrost-api-key").   * writes evals/runs.json as an array of { "example_id": <id>, "output": <model text> } (the shape ax experiments create expects via --file).   * is read-only w.r.t. the repo otherwise; prints a short summary. - Add an "eval" script to package.json scripts: "eval": "tsx evals/run-experiment.ts" (tsx is fine; if not a dep, you may `bun add -d tsx`).  == 5. Update .env.example == After the existing Bifrost block, add a commented "--- Arize (LLM observability + evals) ---" section documenting ARIZE_API_KEY, ARIZE_SPACE_ID, ARIZE_PROJECT_NAME (with =twyne default). Commented out, no real values.  When done, print a concise summary of every file you created/changed. Do not run the eval script or any convex/ax/git commands.

input.description: Read ax profile setup reference

content.content:
# ax Profile Setup

Consult this when authentication fails (401, missing profile, missing API key). Do NOT run these checks proactively.

Use this when there is no profile, or a profile has incorrect settings (wrong API key, wrong region, etc.).

## 1. Inspect the current state

```bash
ax profiles show
```

Look at the output to understand what's configured:
- `API Key: (not set)` or missing → key needs to be created/updated
- No profile output or "No profiles found" → no profile exists yet
- Connected but getting `401 Unauthorized` → key is wrong or expired
- Connected but wrong endpoint/region → region needs to be updated

## 2. Fix a misconfigured profile

If a profile exists but one or more settings are wrong, patch only what's broken.

**Never pass a raw API key value as a flag.** Always reference it via the `ARIZE_API_KEY` environment variable. If the variable is not already set in the shell, instruct the user to set it first, then run the command:

```bash
# If ARIZE_API_KEY is already exported in the shell:
ax profiles update --api-key $ARIZE_API_KEY

# Fix the region (no secret involved — safe to run directly)
ax profiles update --region us-east-1b

# Fix both at once
ax profiles update --api-key $ARIZE_API_KEY --region us-east-1b
```

`update` only changes the fields you specify — all other settings are preserved. If no profile name is given, the active profile is updated.

## 3. Create a new profile

If no profile exists, or if the existing profile needs to point to a completely different setup (different org, different region):

**Always reference the key via `$ARIZE_API_KEY`, never inline a raw value.**

```bash
# Requires ARIZE_API_KEY to be exported in the shell first
ax profiles create --api-key $ARIZE_API_KEY

# Create with a region
ax profiles create --api-key $ARIZE_API_KEY --region us-east-1b

# Create a named profile
ax profiles create work --api-key $ARIZE_API_KEY --region us-east-1b
```

To use a named profile with any `ax` command, add `-p NAME`:
```bash
ax spans export PROJECT -p work
```

## 4. Getting the API key

**Never ask the user to paste their API key into the chat. Never log, echo, or display an API key value.**

If `ARIZE_API_KEY` is not already set, instruct the user to export it in their shell:

```bash
export ARIZE_API_KEY="..."   # user pastes their key here in their own terminal
```

They can find their key at https://app.arize.com by navigating to the settings page. Recommend they create a **scoped service key** (not a personal user key) — service keys are not tied to an individual account and are safer for programmatic use. Keys are space-scoped — make sure they copy the key for the correct space.

Once the user confirms the variable is set, proceed with `ax profiles create --api-key $ARIZE_API_KEY` or `ax profiles update --api-key $ARIZE_API_KEY` as described above.

## 5. Verify

After any create or update:

```bash
ax profiles show
```

content.text: The `ak-` key is rejected by Arize's REST API that `ax` uses. My hypothesis: this is an **ingestion key** (valid for OTLP tracing in Phase A via the `arize-api-key` header) but **not** a REST/CLI key (which `ax` needs for Phase B/C, created separately at app.arize.com/admin → API Keys). Let me test the OTLP ingestion auth directly to confirm Phase A will work with this key.

input.command:
SSL=/etc/pki/tls/certs/ca-bundle.crt
KEY="$(skate get arize_api_key)"
SPACE="$(skate get arize_space_id)"
echo "=== OTLP ingestion auth probe (empty body) ==="
curl -sS -m 20 --cacert "$SSL" -o /tmp/otlp_probe.txt -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" \
  -X POST "https://otlp.arize.com/v1/traces" \
  -H "arize-space-id: $SPACE" \
  -H "arize-api-key: $KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-protobuf" \
  --data-binary ""
echo "--- body ---"; head -c 400 /tmp/otlp_probe.txt; echo
echo
echo "=== compare: bad key should differ ==="
curl -sS -m 20 --cacert "$SSL" -o /dev/null -w "with BAD key: HTTP %{http_code}\n" \
  -X POST "https://otlp.arize.com/v1/traces" \
  -H "arize-space-id: $SPACE" -H "arize-api-key: ak-bogus-000" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-protobuf" --data-binary ""

content.text:
The OTLP empty-body probe is inconclusive (both keys 500 on the missing span). But the `ax` REST result is conclusive: this key is **not valid for the `ax` CLI's management API**. This matches Arize's own docs, which use *two different* credentials:

- **OTLP tracing (Phase A)**: Space ID + **API Key from Space Settings** (ingestion key) — likely what you gave me.
- **`ax` CLI (Phase B/C)**: a separate key from **app.arize.com/admin → API Keys** (REST/service key).

Let me check on crush's progress before I escalate the key question.

[profile]
name = "default"

[auth]
auth_method = "api-key"
api_key = "REDACTED"

[routing]
api_host = "api.arize.com"
api_scheme = "https"
app_host = "app.arize.com"
app_scheme = "https"
otlp_host = "otlp.arize.com"
otlp_scheme = "https"
flight_host = "flight.arize.com"
flight_port = "443"
flight_scheme = "grpc+tls"

[transport]
stream_max_workers = 8
stream_max_queue_bound = 5000
pyarrow_max_chunksize = 10000
max_http_payload_size_mb = 8

[security]
request_verify = true

[storage]
directory = "~/.arize"
cache_enabled = true

[output]
format = "table"

[update]
check_interval_hours = 6.0
enabled = true

input.command:
SSL=/etc/pki/tls/certs/ca-bundle.crt
SVC="$(skate get arize_service_key)"
SPACE="$(skate get arize_space_id)"
for desc_hdr in "Authorization: Bearer $SVC" "arize-api-key: $SVC" "x-api-key: $SVC" "Authorization: $SVC"; do
  code=$(curl -sS -m 15 --cacert "$SSL" -o /tmp/arz.txt -w "%{http_code}" \
    "https://api.arize.com/v1/spaces" -H "$desc_hdr" 2>/dev/null)
  printf "%-32s -> HTTP %s | %s\n" "${desc_hdr%%:*}" "$code" "$(head -c 120 /tmp/arz.txt | tr '\n' ' ')"
done

content.text:
Here's the full picture.

## ✅ Code is done and verified (Phases A + C)

Crush implemented the whole code surface, in scope, deleting nothing. I reviewed it, fixed one dead-variable, and verified:

| Check | Result |
|---|---|
| `convex/arizeTracing.ts` (dormant-by-default OTel→Arize tracer) | ✅ to spec |
| `agents.ts` — telemetry on all 7 `generateText` + `flushArize()` | ✅ |
| `convex tsc --noEmit` | ✅ exit 0 |
| **esbuild bundle** of the OTel proto exporter (the real Phase-A risk) | ✅ exit 0 — bundles in Convex's Node runtime |
| `evals/` harness (reuses canonical `PERSONAS` + `toAgentPersona`) | ✅ runs, builds real prompts, writes `runs.json` |
| `npm run eval` end-to-end | ✅ code path works (failed only on the credential below) |

## 🔑 Three live-credential blockers — all yours to resolve, none are code

1. **Bifrost key revoked.** `BIFROST_API_KEY` in your Convex env (`sk-bf-e056…b96e`) now returns `virtual_key_not_found / revoked`. It worked earlier this session — looks rotated after I flagged it. → The eval (and your *production* persona AI) can't reach Bifrost until this is refreshed.
2. **Arize ingestion key** (`ak-1b…`, Phase A tracing) — couldn't positively confirm (OTLP needs a real span body), but see #3.
3. **Arize service key** (`ak-a5d1…`, for `ax` CLI / Phases B–C scoring) — **401 across REST *and* GraphQL** on `api.arize.com`/`app.arize.com`. Both Arize keys fail identically, which usually means **wrong data region** (your space id decodes to `Space:219:…` — possibly not the default US region) or keys from a different tenant.

I've stored all keys in skate; `ax` 0.25.0 is installed and the profile is configured (just pointing at a region/keys that 401).

## What I need from you

- **Bifrost:** the current (post-rotation) Bifrost API key, so I can update Convex env and re-run the eval. (Without it, production AI is also down.)
- **Arize region:** in the Arize UI, which region is your space in (e.g. the URL host, or Settings → region)? If it's not `app.arize.com`/`api.arize.com`, I'll repoint the `ax` profile. If it *is* the default, the keys may need re-issuing from **app.arize.com/admin → API Keys**.

I deliberately have **not** set `ARIZE_*` in your Convex prod env yet — enabling tracing with bad creds would add per-request flush latency. I'll flip it on the moment the keys check out. Want me to start with the Bifrost key while you check the Arize region?

# ARIZE_PROJECT_NAME=twyne
# Arize is region-sharded; spans must go to the space's region or they 401.
# ARIZE_REGION=ca-central-1a            # us-east-1b | us-central-1a | eu-west-1a | ca-central-1a
# ARIZE_OTLP_ENDPOINT=                  # optional full override of the OTLP URL

# --- Rivet ---
toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Twyne environment variables.
# Copy to `.env.local` (gitignored) for local dev, or set these in your
# Railway service for production. `VITE_*` / `PUBLIC_*` values are inlined
# into the client bundle at BUILD time, so they must be present when the
# build command runs (not just at runtime).

# --- Convex backend (required) ---
# Convex deployment the client talks to. `root.tsx` reads PUBLIC_CONVEX_URL
# first, then falls back to VITE_CONVEX_URL.
VITE_CONVEX_URL=https://your-deployment.convex.cloud
# Convex HTTP actions / site URL (used by auth + published routes).
VITE_CONVEX_SITE_URL=https://your-deployment.convex.site
# Optional alias honored by root.tsx in addition to VITE_CONVEX_URL.
# PUBLIC_CONVEX_URL=https://your-deployment.convex.cloud
# Used by the `npx convex` CLI to target a deployment (dev/codegen only).
CONVEX_DEPLOYMENT=dev:your-deployment
# Production deploy key (Convex dashboard → Settings → Deploy keys). Set this in
# Railway: the build runs `npx convex deploy` with it to push the backend on
# every deploy. If unset, the build ships the frontend only.
# CONVEX_DEPLOY_KEY=prod:your-deployment|xxxxxxxx

# --- Auth (Better Auth) ---
# 32+ char random secret. Generate: `openssl rand -base64 32`
BETTER_AUTH_SECRET=replace-me
# Public origin of the deployed app (used for auth callbacks / cookies).
BETTER_AUTH_URL=https://www.twyne.love
# Canonical site origin (OG/canonical/ATProto redirects).
SITE_URL=https://www.twyne.love

# --- Email (Resend) ---
# Optional: remove the `#` and add your Resend API key to send real
# verification emails. Without it, OTPs are only logged to the console.
# RESEND_API_KEY=re_xxx
# RESEND_FROM_EMAIL=Twyne <support@twyne.love>

# --- AI providers ---
# Twyne is BYOK: end users paste their own API keys in Settings, stored
# locally in the browser. These server-side keys are optional and only
# needed if you wire a server-side provider.
# ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=
# OPENAI_API_KEY=

# --- Voice narration ---
# Optional: Pro-tier hosted voice uses OpenAI speech generation server-side.
# If unset, hosted voice falls back to OPENAI_API_KEY. BYOK voice uses the
# writer's browser-stored provider key and does not need these server vars.
# VOICE_OPENAI_API_KEY=
# VOICE_OPENAI_MODEL=gpt-4o-mini-tts
# VOICE_OPENAI_VOICE=alloy

# --- Bifrost (managed AI gateway) ---
# Optional: route all server-side AI calls through a Bifrost instance.
# When BIFROST_BASE_URL + BIFROST_API_KEY are set, Bifrost takes priority
# over Anthropic / OpenAI / Rivet in the provider chain.
# BIFROST_BASE_URL=https://bifrost-production-19dd.up.railway.app/openai/v1
# BIFROST_API_KEY=sk_bf_xxx
# BIFROST_DEFAULT_MODEL=neuralwatt/qwen3.5-397b-fast

# --- Arize (LLM observability + evals) ---
# OpenInference tracing for every server-side generateText call in
# convex/agents.ts, plus a destination for offline experiment scoring.
# Set ARIZE_API_KEY + ARIZE_SPACE_ID in the Convex deployment env (skate
# has the values); without them the tracer stays dormant and flushArize()
# is a no-op. ARIZE_PROJECT_NAME is the Arize project the spans land in
# and matches the model_id resource attribute (default: twyne).
# ARIZE_API_KEY=
# ARIZE_SPACE_ID=
# ARIZE_PROJECT_NAME=twyne

# --- Rivet ---
# Optional: self-hosted or managed Rivet agentOS endpoint.
# RIVET_ENDPOINT=https://your-rivet-instance.com
# RIVET_TOKEN=rivet_xxx
# RIVET_MODEL=anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5

# --- Runtime ---
# Injected automatically by Railway; defaults to 3000 locally.
# PORT=3000

# --- PostHog analytics, feature flags, and AI eval capture ---
# Public browser token + host. PostHog flags are the source of truth for:
#   twyne-pricing   -> /pricing + Creem checkout UI
#   twyne-local-ai  -> desktop-only LiteRT provider UI
# PUBLIC_POSTHOG_KEY=phc_xxx
# PUBLIC_POSTHOG_HOST=https://us.i.posthog.com
# Set false to evaluate flags but suppress analytics/session capture.
# PUBLIC_POSTHOG_CAPTURE=true

# Convex/server-side AI observability capture for PostHog AI evals.
# This can be the same project token as PUBLIC_POSTHOG_KEY, set in Convex env.
# POSTHOG_PROJECT_API_KEY=phc_xxx
# POSTHOG_HOST=https://us.i.posthog.com
# POSTHOG_CAPTURE=true

# --- Feature flag fallbacks (used only when PostHog is not configured/loaded) ---
# PUBLIC_FEATURE_PRICING=true
# PUBLIC_FEATURE_LOCAL_AI=true

# --- Creem payments (only needed when twyne-pricing is enabled) ---
# Public: the Pro plan's Creem product id (safe to ship in the client).
# PUBLIC_CREEM_PRODUCT_PRO=prod_xxx
# Secret (set in Convex, not the client): API key + webhook signing secret.
# CREEM_API_KEY=creem_xxx
# Optional explicit API endpoint. Test keys automatically select Creem's test API.
# CREEM_API_BASE=https://test-api.creem.io/v1
# CREEM_WEBHOOK_SECRET=whsec_xxx
# Where Creem returns the buyer after checkout.
# CREEM_SUCCESS_URL=https://twyne.love/pricing?status=success
# Server-side allowlist of Creem product ids that grant Pro (comma-separated).
# Must be set as a Convex secret; checkout is rejected until it contains the
# Pro product id(s). Stops a cheaper/unexpected product id from granting Pro.
# CREEM_PRO_PRODUCT_IDS=prod_pro,prod_pro_yearly

# --- Desktop local model (Electrobun build only) ---
# Bundle + launch the native LiteRT-LM server in the desktop build.
# TWYNE_DESKTOP_LOCAL_AI=true
# Path to the Gemma 4 E4B LiteRT model file bundled into the desktop app.
# LOCAL_MODEL_PATH=./desktop/models/gemma-4-e4b.litertlm

content.text:
The Canada region was the whole Arize problem — keys were valid all along. Here's where we are:

## ✅ Now working / verified
- **Arize CLI** (`ax`) authenticates to `ca-central-1a` — `spaces list` shows your **"kmannoel Space"**.
- **Phase A tracing, live-verified**: a real span exported cleanly to `otlp.ca-central-1a.arize.com` and was accepted. Code is region-aware (`ARIZE_REGION` env, Canada default), typechecks, and bundles for Convex.
- **Arize env vars set on the dev deployment** (`acoustic-meerkat-189`): `ARIZE_API_KEY` (ingestion), `ARIZE_SPACE_ID`, `ARIZE_PROJECT_NAME=twyne`, `ARIZE_REGION=ca-central-1a`.
- **Phase C harness** code complete and confirmed executing.

## 🔑 One blocker remains: the Bifrost key
The `BIFROST_API_KEY` in your Convex env is **revoked** (`virtual_key_not_found`) — it worked earlier this session, so it looks rotated. This blocks three things at once:
1. **Phase C** eval generation (the harness 401s on every call),
2. **Phase B** — the Arize judge integration reuses Bifrost, so evaluator runs would fail,
3. your **production persona AI** (same key).

**Can you give me the current Bifrost key?** I'll update Convex env, re-run the eval to produce real `runs.json`, then build the Phase B judge integration + correctness/faithfulness evaluators and the offline experiment in Arize.

## One decision for you

13	
14	**Railway service `twyne` (env `production`)** — frontend build/runtime only. `VITE_*` are baked in at BUILD time. Set: `VITE_CONVEX_URL`, `VITE_CONVEX_SITE_URL`, `PUBLIC_POSTHOG_KEY`, `PUBLIC_POSTHOG_HOST`, `CONVEX_DEPLOY_KEY`, `SITE_URL`. (`BETTER_AUTH_SECRET`/`BETTER_AUTH_URL` were also set here but are **unused** on Railway — see below.)
15	
16	**Convex deployment env** — everything the `convex/*.ts` functions read at runtime: `BETTER_AUTH_SECRET` (Better Auth runs *inside* Convex — `convex/auth.ts` is the only reader; the Railway copy is dead), `SITE_URL` (auth redirects; defaults to `localhost:5173` if unset!), `BIFROST_*` (hosted AI), `RESEND_*` (email), `POSTHOG_PROJECT_API_KEY`+`POSTHOG_HOST` (server AI-observability), and when pricing is on `CREEM_API_KEY`/`CREEM_WEBHOOK_SECRET`/`CREEM_SUCCESS_URL`. `CONVEX_SITE_URL` is auto-provided by Convex — don't set it.
17	
18	**fnox vault** (`fnox list`) holds the real LLM/provider + infra secrets: `anthropic_api_key`, `openai_api_key`, `gemini_api_key`, `bifrost_api_key`/`bifrost_gateway_url`, `resend`, cloudflare, tailscale, porkbun, google_oauth, etc. The Convex `BIFROST_*`/`RESEND_*` values came from here. Twyne is BYOK so server LLM keys are optional (Bifrost is the configured hosted path).
19	
20	**Gotchas:**
21	- A Convex **deploy key** (`CONVEX_DEPLOY_KEY`) can push code but **cannot** write env vars → `403 WriteEnvironmentVariables`. Set Convex env with an authenticated session instead: `env -u CONVEX_DEPLOY_KEY bunx convex env set NAME val --prod`.
22	- Railway CLI link has no service selected by default — pass `--service twyne` to every `railway variables` call.

file.content:
---
name: twyne-prod-env-split
description: Where each prod secret lives — Railway (frontend) vs Convex deployment vs fnox vault
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: ff27101b-10f6-4f4c-b11f-d112ba690dba
---

Twyne prod env vars are split across **two** systems, plus **fnox** as the secret source. Getting the side wrong = silently broken prod. See [[railway-deploy-setup]].

**Prod Convex deployment: `hushed-aardvark-411`** (`https://hushed-aardvark-411.convex.cloud`, site `…convex.site`).

**Railway service `twyne` (env `production`)** — frontend build/runtime only. `VITE_*` are baked in at BUILD time. Set: `VITE_CONVEX_URL`, `VITE_CONVEX_SITE_URL`, `PUBLIC_POSTHOG_KEY`, `PUBLIC_POSTHOG_HOST`, `CONVEX_DEPLOY_KEY`, `SITE_URL`. (`BETTER_AUTH_SECRET`/`BETTER_AUTH_URL` were also set here but are **unused** on Railway — see below.)

**Convex deployment env** — everything the `convex/*.ts` functions read at runtime: `BETTER_AUTH_SECRET` (Better Auth runs *inside* Convex — `convex/auth.ts` is the only reader; the Railway copy is dead), `SITE_URL` (auth redirects; defaults to `localhost:5173` if unset!), `BIFROST_*` (hosted AI), `RESEND_*` (email), `POSTHOG_PROJECT_API_KEY`+`POSTHOG_HOST` (server AI-observability), and when pricing is on `CREEM_API_KEY`/`CREEM_WEBHOOK_SECRET`/`CREEM_SUCCESS_URL`. `CONVEX_SITE_URL` is auto-provided by Convex — don't set it.

**fnox vault** (`fnox list`) holds the real LLM/provider + infra secrets: `anthropic_api_key`, `openai_api_key`, `gemini_api_key`, `bifrost_api_key`/`bifrost_gateway_url`, `resend`, cloudflare, tailscale, porkbun, google_oauth, etc. The Convex `BIFROST_*`/`RESEND_*` values came from here. Twyne is BYOK so server LLM keys are optional (Bifrost is the configured hosted path).

**Gotchas:**
- A Convex **deploy key** (`CONVEX_DEPLOY_KEY`) can push code but **cannot** write env vars → `403 WriteEnvironmentVariables`. Set Convex env with an authenticated session instead: `env -u CONVEX_DEPLOY_KEY bunx convex env set NAME val --prod`.
- Railway CLI link has no service selected by default — pass `--service twyne` to every `railway variables` call.
