content.content: Updated task #6 status

28	**Phase B blocker — AI integration create is 403 for the API key.** The `ax`/SDK `POST /v2/ai-integrations` (alpha) endpoint returns **403 Forbidden** for the user's developer API key (`is_developer=True`, predefined **admin** role) — tried CLI account-wide, minimal anthropic, and SDK with explicit space+org `scopings`; all 403. Same key READS integrations and WRITES datasets fine, so it's specific to this create action/endpoint, NOT a plan gate and NOT the bifrost key. LLM-as-judge `create-template-evaluator` REQUIRES `--ai-integration-id` (no inline judge provider), so the integration is a hard prerequisite. Org id = `REDACTED`, space = `U3BhY2U6MjE5OkI1UjA=`. Resolution paths: (a) create "bifrost-judge" in the Arize UI (Custom provider, base url https://bifrost.dio.computer/openai/v1, header `x-bifrost-api-key`=<bifrost key>, model `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`); or (b) `ax auth login` (OAuth session, acts as the user — may have create perms the dev key lacks). Then create correctness + faithfulness-to-draft template evaluators (`--ai-integration-id <id>`, temp 0) + a continuous task. NOTE: judge calls to bifrost must use header auth only (NO Authorization bearer) — but the UI/integration form may inject a bearer; verify the judge actually runs.
29	
30	Implementation was done by the **crush** CLI (`crush run`, no --yolo — the auto-mode classifier blocks `--yolo`), scoped tightly per file; reviewed by me. See [[twyne-prod-env-split]].
31

file.content:
**Phase B blocker — AI integration create is 403 for the API key.** The `ax`/SDK `POST /v2/ai-integrations` (alpha) endpoint returns **403 Forbidden** for the user's developer API key (`is_developer=True`, predefined **admin** role) — tried CLI account-wide, minimal anthropic, and SDK with explicit space+org `scopings`; all 403. Same key READS integrations and WRITES datasets fine, so it's specific to this create action/endpoint, NOT a plan gate and NOT the bifrost key. LLM-as-judge `create-template-evaluator` REQUIRES `--ai-integration-id` (no inline judge provider), so the integration is a hard prerequisite. Org id = `REDACTED`, space = `U3BhY2U6MjE5OkI1UjA=`. Resolution paths: (a) create "bifrost-judge" in the Arize UI (Custom provider, base url https://bifrost.dio.computer/openai/v1, header `x-bifrost-api-key`=<bifrost key>, model `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`); or (b) `ax auth login` (OAuth session, acts as the user — may have create perms the dev key lacks). Then create correctness + faithfulness-to-draft template evaluators (`--ai-integration-id <id>`, temp 0) + a continuous task. NOTE: judge calls to bifrost must use header auth only (NO Authorization bearer) — but the UI/integration form may inject a bearer; verify the judge actually runs.

Implementation was done by the **crush** CLI (`crush run`, no --yolo — the auto-mode classifier blocks `--yolo`), scoped tightly per file; reviewed by me. See [[twyne-prod-env-split]].

input.old_string: Implementation was done by the **crush** CLI (`crush run`, no --yolo — the auto-mode classifier blocks `--yolo`), scoped tightly per file; reviewed by me. See [[twyne-prod-env-split]].
input.new_string:
**Phase B — DONE + verified (judge proven; native Arize task blocked on UI auth flip).** Two template evaluators exist in the space: `faithfulness-to-draft` (`RXZhbHVhdG9yOjMyOmJNclQ=`, grounded=1/generic=0) and `feedback-helpfulness` (`RXZhbHVhdG9yOjMzOmtocmk=`, good=1/poor=0), both judge model `neuralwatt/kimi-k2.6` via integration `bifrost` (`REDACTED`). Experiment `bifrost-neuralwatt-2026-06-29` (`RXhwZXJpbWVudDoyNDY6bnVTZg==`, dataset `twyne-evals`) has 7 real runs. **The native eval task (`T25saW5lVGFzazo4MTp5MnlG`) fails all 14 (7×2) because the integration's `auth_type: default` injects `Authorization: Bearer` to Bifrost → upstream 401.** Fix is one UI change: integration → auth type **proxy_with_headers** (CLI `ax ai-integrations update --auth-type proxy_with_headers` is 403 for the dev key — same restriction as create; UI only). `ax experiments annotate-runs` can't backfill the local scores either: the CLI **masks experiment run IDs to `EXP_ID_*`** in export/list-runs, so there's no usable `record_id`.

Because both native write-paths need the UI, the judge is committed in-repo as **`evals/judge.ts`** (`npm run eval:judge`, tsx): reads `evals/runs.json` + `evals/dataset.jsonl`, applies the two rubrics verbatim through Bifrost with header-only auth, writes `evals/scores.json` (gitignored). Verified end-to-end: 7/7 grounded + 7/7 good on real outputs, and a negative control (generic boilerplate feedback) correctly returns generic(0)/poor(0) — the judge discriminates, not rubber-stamps. This proves the LLM-as-judge + gateway work; the only thing the UI auth flip adds is native scores landing in the Arize UI/continuous task.

Implementation was done by the **crush** CLI (`crush run`, no --yolo — the auto-mode classifier blocks `--yolo`), scoped tightly per file; reviewed by me. See [[twyne-prod-env-split]].

toolUseResult.originalFile:
---
name: arize-evals-integration
description: "Arize eval/tracing integration — Canada region, OTLP tracer, ax CLI, key types"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: 6e1b765d-5eab-471f-ac93-97d36f0ec721
---

Arize observability + evals were added to Twyne (server AI in `convex/agents.ts`). Skills live under `.agents/skills/arize-*` (gitignored); CLI is `ax` (uv tool `arize-ax-cli`, v0.25.0).

**Region is `ca-central-1a` (Canada) — this is the #1 gotcha.** Arize is region-sharded: hosts are `api.<region>.arize.com` / `otlp.<region>.arize.com`. The default US hosts (`api.arize.com`) return a misleading **401 "invalid API key"** for a CA space even with a valid key. Fix: `ax profiles update --region ca-central-1a`. Space = "kmannoel Space" (`U3BhY2U6MjE5OkI1UjA=`), owner kmannoel@gmail.com.

**Two key types, both stored in skate** (`skate get <name>`):
- `arize_api_key` (`ak-1b…`) — ingestion key; used as OTLP `arize-api-key` header for tracing.
- `arize_service_key` (`ak-a5d1…`) — admin/service key for the `ax` REST/GraphQL CLI.
- `arize_space_id` — the space id above.
Both keys authenticate to OTLP; tracing uses the least-privilege ingestion key.

**Phase A (live tracing) — DONE + verified.** `convex/arizeTracing.ts`: dormant-when-unconfigured singleton `NodeTracerProvider` + `OpenInferenceSimpleSpanProcessor` (synchronous per-span export — NOT Batch, which drops spans on serverless freeze) → OTLP. Region-aware: `ARIZE_REGION` (default `ca-central-1a`) or full `ARIZE_OTLP_ENDPOINT` override. `agents.ts` adds `experimental_telemetry` to all 7 `generateText` sites + `await flushArize()` before every return/throw. Verified: convex tsc, esbuild bundle, and a real span exported + accepted by the CA OTLP endpoint. The OTLP collector REQUIRES the `model_id` resource attr (set to ARIZE_PROJECT_NAME) or it 500s "model_id ... required".

**Env set on BOTH deployments** (dev `acoustic-meerkat-189`, prod `hushed-aardvark-411`): `ARIZE_API_KEY`, `ARIZE_SPACE_ID`, `ARIZE_PROJECT_NAME=twyne`, `ARIZE_REGION=ca-central-1a`. NOTE: env alone doesn't enable tracing — the instrumented code must be **deployed** (`npx convex deploy`) first. Interactive `npx convex env set --prod` works (the deploy-key 403 in [[twyne-prod-env-split]] only applies to deploy keys).

**Phase C (offline harness) — code DONE.** `evals/dataset.jsonl` + `evals/run-experiment.ts` (`npm run eval`, uses tsx). Reuses real `PERSONAS` (`src/utils/personas.ts`) + `toAgentPersona` (`convex/agentPrompts.ts:341`) + prompt builders, calls Bifrost, writes `evals/runs.json` in `ax experiments create --file` shape.

**Bifrost auth gotcha (NOT a bad key — key is valid).** Bifrost at bifrost.dio.computer uses *provider-key passthrough* via the `x-bifrost-api-key` header; it has NO "virtual key" configured. So: `x-bifrost-api-key` alone → 200. But ANY `Authorization: Bearer …` header makes Bifrost (a) treat the bearer as a virtual-key lookup (→ 401 `virtual_key_not_found`) or (b) forward the bearer upstream to the real provider (→ `provider API error (status 401)` from neuralwatt). Two bugs found+fixed this way: (1) `evals/run-experiment.ts` sent a stray `Authorization: Bearer` — removed. (2) **Pre-existing prod bug** in `convex/agents.ts`: `createOpenAI({apiKey:"bifrost-dummy"})` always injects `Authorization: Bearer bifrost-dummy`, which Bifrost forwarded to neuralwatt → 401 → server AI silently fell back to the LOCAL generator. Fixed with a `fetch:` wrapper on the bifrost provider that strips the `authorization` header. Eval now 7/7 green; verified the AI-SDK path returns real output. `runs.json` is gitignored.

**Phase B blocker — AI integration create is 403 for the API key.** The `ax`/SDK `POST /v2/ai-integrations` (alpha) endpoint returns **403 Forbidden** for the user's developer API key (`is_developer=True`, predefined **admin** role) — tried CLI account-wide, minimal anthropic, and SDK with explicit space+org `scopings`; all 403. Same key READS integrations and WRITES datasets fine, so it's specific to this create action/endpoint, NOT a plan gate and NOT the bifrost key. LLM-as-judge `create-template-evaluator` REQUIRES `--ai-integration-id` (no inline judge provider), so the integration is a hard prerequisite. Org id = `REDACTED`, space = `U3BhY2U6MjE5OkI1UjA=`. Resolution paths: (a) create "bifrost-judge" in the Arize UI (Custom provider, base url https://bifrost.dio.computer/openai/v1, header `x-bifrost-api-key`=<bifrost key>, model `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`); or (b) `ax auth login` (OAuth session, acts as the user — may have create perms the dev key lacks). Then create correctness + faithfulness-to-draft template evaluators (`--ai-integration-id <id>`, temp 0) + a continuous task. NOTE: judge calls to bifrost must use header auth only (NO Authorization bearer) — but the UI/integration form may inject a bearer; verify the judge actually runs.

Implementation was done by the **crush** CLI (`crush run`, no --yolo — the auto-mode classifier blocks `--yolo`), scoped tightly per file; reviewed by me. See [[twyne-prod-env-split]].

lines: **Phase B blocker — AI integration create is 403 for the API key.** The `ax`/SDK `POST /v2/ai-integrations` (alpha) endpoint returns **403 Forbidden** for the user's developer API key (`is_developer=True`, predefined **admin** role) — tried CLI account-wide, minimal anthropic, and SDK with explicit space+org `scopings`; all 403. Same key READS integrations and WRITES datasets fine, so it's specific to this create action/endpoint, NOT a plan gate and NOT the bifrost key. LLM-as-judge `create-template-evaluator` REQUIRES `--ai-integration-id` (no inline judge provider), so the integration is a hard prerequisite. Org id = `REDACTED`, space = `U3BhY2U6MjE5OkI1UjA=`. Resolution paths: (a) create "bifrost-judge" in the Arize UI (Custom provider, base url https://bifrost.dio.computer/openai/v1, header `x-bifrost-api-key`=<bifrost key>, model `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`); or (b) `ax auth login` (OAuth session, acts as the user — may have create perms the dev key lacks). Then create correctness + faithfulness-to-draft template evaluators (`--ai-integration-id <id>`, temp 0) + a continuous task. NOTE: judge calls to bifrost must use header auth only (NO Authorization bearer) — but the UI/integration form may inject a bearer; verify the judge actually runs.

+**Phase B — DONE + verified (judge proven; native Arize task blocked on UI auth flip).** Two template evaluators exist in the space: `faithfulness-to-draft` (`RXZhbHVhdG9yOjMyOmJNclQ=`, grounded=1/generic=0) and `feedback-helpfulness` (`RXZhbHVhdG9yOjMzOmtocmk=`, good=1/poor=0), both judge model `neuralwatt/kimi-k2.6` via integration `bifrost` (`REDACTED`). Experiment `bifrost-neuralwatt-2026-06-29` (`RXhwZXJpbWVudDoyNDY6bnVTZg==`, dataset `twyne-evals`) has 7 real runs. **The native eval task (`T25saW5lVGFzazo4MTp5MnlG`) fails all 14 (7×2) because the integration's `auth_type: default` injects `Authorization: Bearer` to Bifrost → upstream 401.** Fix is one UI change: integration → auth type **proxy_with_headers** (CLI `ax ai-integrations update --auth-type proxy_with_headers` is 403 for the dev key — same restriction as create; UI only). `ax experiments annotate-runs` can't backfill the local scores either: the CLI **masks experiment run IDs to `EXP_ID_*`** in export/list-runs, so there's no usable `record_id`.

+Because both native write-paths need the UI, the judge is committed in-repo as **`evals/judge.ts`** (`npm run eval:judge`, tsx): reads `evals/runs.json` + `evals/dataset.jsonl`, applies the two rubrics verbatim through Bifrost with header-only auth, writes `evals/scores.json` (gitignored). Verified end-to-end: 7/7 grounded + 7/7 good on real outputs, and a negative control (generic boilerplate feedback) correctly returns generic(0)/poor(0) — the judge discriminates, not rubber-stamps. This proves the LLM-as-judge + gateway work; the only thing the UI auth flip adds is native scores landing in the Arize UI/continuous task.
Implementation was done by the **crush** CLI (`crush run`, no --yolo — the auto-mode classifier blocks `--yolo`), scoped tightly per file; reviewed by me. See [[twyne-prod-env-split]].

# Twyne environment variables.
# Copy to `.env.local` (gitignored) for local dev, or set these in your
# Railway service for production. `VITE_*` / `PUBLIC_*` values are inlined
# into the client bundle at BUILD time, so they must be present when the
# build command runs (not just at runtime).

# --- Convex backend (required) ---
# Convex deployment the client talks to. `root.tsx` reads PUBLIC_CONVEX_URL
# first, then falls back to VITE_CONVEX_URL.
VITE_CONVEX_URL=https://your-deployment.convex.cloud
# Convex HTTP actions / site URL (used by auth + published routes).
VITE_CONVEX_SITE_URL=https://your-deployment.convex.site
# Optional alias honored by root.tsx in addition to VITE_CONVEX_URL.
# PUBLIC_CONVEX_URL=https://your-deployment.convex.cloud
# Used by the `npx convex` CLI to target a deployment (dev/codegen only).
CONVEX_DEPLOYMENT=dev:your-deployment
# Production deploy key (Convex dashboard → Settings → Deploy keys). Set this in
# Railway: the build runs `npx convex deploy` with it to push the backend on
# every deploy. If unset, the build ships the frontend only.
# CONVEX_DEPLOY_KEY=prod:your-deployment|xxxxxxxx

# --- Auth (Better Auth) ---
# 32+ char random secret. Generate: `openssl rand -base64 32`
BETTER_AUTH_SECRET=replace-me
# Public origin of the deployed app (used for auth callbacks / cookies).
BETTER_AUTH_URL=https://www.twyne.love
# Canonical site origin (OG/canonical/ATProto redirects).
SITE_URL=https://www.twyne.love

# --- Email (Resend) ---
# Optional: remove the `#` and add your Resend API key to send real
# verification emails. Without it, OTPs are only logged to the console.
# RESEND_API_KEY=re_xxx
# RESEND_FROM_EMAIL=Twyne <support@twyne.love>

# --- AI providers ---
# Twyne is BYOK: end users paste their own API keys in Settings, stored
# locally in the browser. These server-side keys are optional and only
# needed if you wire a server-side provider.
# ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=
# OPENAI_API_KEY=

# --- Voice narration ---
# Optional: Pro-tier hosted voice uses OpenAI speech generation server-side.
# If unset, hosted voice falls back to OPENAI_API_KEY. BYOK voice uses the
# writer's browser-stored provider key and does not need these server vars.
# VOICE_OPENAI_API_KEY=
# VOICE_OPENAI_MODEL=gpt-4o-mini-tts
# VOICE_OPENAI_VOICE=alloy

# --- Bifrost (managed AI gateway) ---
# Optional: route all server-side AI calls through a Bifrost instance.
# When BIFROST_BASE_URL + BIFROST_API_KEY are set, Bifrost takes priority
# over Anthropic / OpenAI / Rivet in the provider chain.
# BIFROST_BASE_URL=https://bifrost-production-19dd.up.railway.app/openai/v1
# BIFROST_API_KEY=sk_bf_xxx
# BIFROST_DEFAULT_MODEL=neuralwatt/qwen3.5-397b-fast

# --- Arize (LLM observability + evals) ---
# OpenInference tracing for every server-side generateText call in
# convex/agents.ts, plus a destination for offline experiment scoring.
# Set ARIZE_API_KEY + ARIZE_SPACE_ID in the Convex deployment env (skate
# has the values); without them the tracer stays dormant and flushArize()
# is a no-op. ARIZE_PROJECT_NAME is the Arize project the spans land in
# and matches the model_id resource attribute (default: twyne).
# ARIZE_API_KEY=
# ARIZE_SPACE_ID=
# ARIZE_PROJECT_NAME=twyne
# Arize is region-sharded; spans must go to the space's region or they 401.
# ARIZE_REGION=ca-central-1a            # us-east-1b | us-central-1a | eu-west-1a | ca-central-1a
# ARIZE_OTLP_ENDPOINT=                  # optional full override of the OTLP URL

# --- Rivet ---
# Optional: self-hosted or managed Rivet agentOS endpoint.
# RIVET_ENDPOINT=https://your-rivet-instance.com
# RIVET_TOKEN=rivet_xxx
# RIVET_MODEL=anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5

# --- Runtime ---
# Injected automatically by Railway; defaults to 3000 locally.
# PORT=3000

# --- PostHog analytics, feature flags, and AI eval capture ---
# Public browser token + host. PostHog flags are the source of truth for:
#   twyne-pricing   -> /pricing + Creem checkout UI
#   twyne-local-ai  -> desktop-only LiteRT provider UI
# PUBLIC_POSTHOG_KEY=phc_xxx
# PUBLIC_POSTHOG_HOST=https://us.i.posthog.com
# Set false to evaluate flags but suppress analytics/session capture.

# Convex/server-side AI observability capture for PostHog AI evals.

**What's proven:** Both evaluators produce correct, discriminating scores through the Bifrost gateway with the `neuralwatt/kimi-k2.6` judge:

| case | faithfulness-to-draft | feedback-helpfulness |
|------|----------------------|---------------------|

| negative control (generic boilerplate) | **generic (0)** | **poor (0)** |

The negative control is the key result — the judge correctly fails generic feedback, so the 7/7 on real outputs is signal, not rubber-stamping.

**What I built/changed:**
- `evals/judge.ts` + `npm run eval:judge` — reads `evals/runs.json` + `evals/dataset.jsonl`, applies both rubrics (verbatim copies of the Arize templates) through Bifrost with **header-only auth**, writes `evals/scores.json`. Ran end-to-end successfully.

The Arize eval task fails all 14 (7×2) because the `bifrost` AI integration has `auth_type: default`, which injects `Authorization: Bearer` to Bifrost → upstream 401 (the same bug we fixed in production). The CLI fix is **403 for your dev key** (same restriction as integration-create), so it needs the UI:

You are adding a new offline evaluation to the Twyne repo (cwd is the repo root). This extends an existing eval harness under `evals/`. Follow the existing files' style closely.

STUDY FIRST (read these, do not modify them):
- `evals/run-experiment.ts` and `evals/judge.ts` — the existing harness for persona feedback. Match their conventions: Node/TS run via `tsx`, `node:fs`/`node:path` imports, header-only Bifrost auth, `case_id` keys, a `main().catch()` tail, writing a `*-scores.json` artifact.
- `convex/agentPrompts.ts` — reuse the REAL prompt builders. You will import: `buildSystemPrompt`, `buildUserPrompt`, `buildRubricReviewSystemPrompt`, `buildRubricReviewPrompt`, `toAgentPersona`, and types `AgentPersona`, `AgentRequest`.
- `src/utils/personas.ts` — exports `PERSONAS`. Persona ids: devil, angel, scholar, editor, reader.
- `convex/agents.ts` `judgeDraft` action (around line 1048) — this is the production rubric-judge. Note the exact JUDGE TASK suffix it appends and that it parses JSON `{"score": <int 1-10>, "rationale": "<one sentence>"}` at temperature 0.2, maxOutputTokens 220.

GOAL: a rubric evaluation with two task types, both replaying the REAL production prompts through the Bifrost gateway, then scoring the outputs with LLM-as-judge rubrics.

CREATE these files:

1) `evals/rubric.jsonl` — one JSON object per line, each with `case_id` (string) and `task` (string).
   - For `task: "rubric-judge"` rows, also: `persona` (a persona id) and `draftText` (string).
     Include at least 4 rows. CRUCIALLY include a discrimination pair on the SAME persona:
       - one STRONG, specific, well-argued draft (should earn a higher score), and
       - one WEAK, generic, repetitive/filler draft (should earn a low score).
     Make drafts 3-6 sentences. Use a couple of different personas (e.g. editor, scholar, devil).
   - For `task: "rubric-review"` rows, also carry the inputs `buildRubricReviewPrompt` needs:
     `combined` (int /100), `grade` (string like "B-"), `judgeMean` (number /10), `staticTotal` (number /10),
     `judges` (array of {personaId, score, rationale}), `staticFeedback` (array of strings), and `draftText`.
     Include at least 2 rows with realistic, internally-consistent numbers.

2) `evals/run-rubric.ts` — the harness. For each row in `evals/rubric.jsonl`:
   - Build a Bifrost call helper with HEADER-ONLY auth. CRITICAL: the only auth header is
     `"x-bifrost-api-key": process.env.BIFROST_API_KEY`. NEVER send an `Authorization`/`Bearer`
     header — Bifrost misreads a bearer as a virtual-key lookup and 401s. (See the comment in
     `evals/judge.ts`.) POST to `${BIFROST_BASE_URL}/chat/completions`.
   - Read `BIFROST_BASE_URL`, `BIFROST_API_KEY`, and `JUDGE_MODEL` (default
     "neuralwatt/kimi-k2.6") from env, exactly like `evals/judge.ts`.
   - task "rubric-judge": GENERATE with system=`buildSystemPrompt(persona)`,
     prompt=`buildUserPrompt({persona, brief:null, draftText, instruction:"feedback"})` + the
     JUDGE TASK suffix copied VERBATIM from `convex/agents.ts` judgeDraft (the block beginning
     "\n\nJUDGE TASK: As ${persona.name}, give the draft a single integer score from 1 to 10..."
     through the JSON shape line). Use generation temperature 0.2 to mirror production. Parse the
     JSON `{score, rationale}` (tolerate code fences / surrounding text by extracting the first
     {...}). Then SCORE it:
       * structural check (no LLM): `score` is an integer in [1,10] -> score_in_range true/false.
       * rationale-grounded judge (LLM, temperature 0): does the rationale reference specifics of
         THIS draft (grounded=1) vs generic boilerplate (generic=0)? Reuse the same grounded/generic
         rubric wording style as `evals/judge.ts` faithfulness.
     After all rubric-judge rows, if a persona has both a STRONG and WEAK draft, assert the strong
     score > weak score and report each pair as discrimination pass/fail (do not throw; print and
     count).
   - task "rubric-review": GENERATE with system=`buildRubricReviewSystemPrompt()`,
     prompt=`buildRubricReviewPrompt({combined, grade, judgeMean, staticTotal, judges, staticFeedback, brief:null, draftText})`.
     temperature 0.4. Then SCORE with two LLM judges (temperature 0):
       * faithful-to-grade: does the review respect the GIVEN grade/scores and NOT invent new numeric
         scores of its own? labels faithful=1 / invented=0.
       * has-revision-plan: does it close with a concrete, prioritized revision plan (multiple
         specific steps)? labels present=1 / absent=0.
   - Each LLM judge must instruct the model to reply with ONLY a JSON object
     {"label": "...", "explanation": "..."} and parse defensively (mirror `evals/judge.ts`).
   - Write `evals/rubric-scores.json` (pretty JSON array). Print a per-row line and a summary
     (e.g. rationale grounded X/Y, score_in_range X/Y, discrimination pairs passed, faithful-to-grade,
     has-revision-plan). Set process.exitCode=1 on hard failures (parse failures, network errors),
     NOT on a low rubric score.

   after the existing `"eval:judge"` entry).

   runs.json and scores.json are already ignored there — match that).

input.content:
You are adding a new offline evaluation to the Twyne repo (cwd is the repo root). This extends an existing eval harness under `evals/`. Match the existing files' style.

STUDY FIRST (read, do not modify):
- `evals/judge.ts` — the existing LLM-as-judge harness. Match its conventions: Node/TS run via `tsx`,
  `node:fs`/`node:path`, HEADER-ONLY Bifrost auth, env vars `BIFROST_BASE_URL` / `BIFROST_API_KEY` /
  `JUDGE_MODEL` (default "neuralwatt/kimi-k2.6"), defensive JSON parsing, a `main().catch()` tail,
  writing a `*-scores.json` artifact.
- `convex/research.ts` — the Apparatus source provider. `searchSources({query, context})` returns
  `{ results: Source[] }` where `Source = { title, url, snippet, author?, publisher?, date?, why? }`.
  The search itself is NOT an LLM call (it's a provider/local-stub), so the evaluable quality is
  whether returned sources are RELEVANT to the writer's query + context. You will import the
  `Source` type from `../convex/research`.

WHAT THIS EVAL DOES: a source-RELEVANCE judge. Given a research query + context and a set of
candidate sources, an LLM judge decides each source is "relevant" or "irrelevant" to the query.
We compare the judge's labels to ground-truth labels carried in the dataset and report accuracy.
This tests the apparatus's core promise (surfacing on-target sources) deterministically and offline,
without needing the TinyFish provider key or Convex.

CREATE these files:

1) `evals/apparatus.jsonl` — one JSON object per line, each with:
   `case_id` (string), `query` (string), `context` (string; the brief's "audience · goal" style),
   and `candidateSources`: an array of objects shaped like `Source` PLUS a ground-truth boolean
   `relevant`. i.e. `{ title, url, snippet, publisher?, date?, relevant }`.
   Provide at least 4 case rows. In EACH row include a MIX of clearly relevant and clearly
   irrelevant sources (at least one of each) so the judge has to discriminate. Make queries/contexts
   resemble real Twyne writing topics (e.g. open-source maintainer burnout; AI adoption in
   enterprises; the epistemics of LLM "knowledge"). Snippets should be 1-2 sentences. Aim for ~3-5
   candidate sources per row.

2) `evals/run-apparatus.ts`:
   - Bifrost call helper with HEADER-ONLY auth. CRITICAL: only auth header is
     `"x-bifrost-api-key": process.env.BIFROST_API_KEY`. NEVER send `Authorization`/`Bearer`
     (Bifrost 401s on a bearer). POST `${BIFROST_BASE_URL}/chat/completions`, temperature 0.
   - For each candidate source in each row, ask the judge (temperature 0): given the QUERY and
     CONTEXT, is THIS source relevant to the writer's research need? The judge must reply with ONLY
     a JSON object {"label": "relevant"|"irrelevant", "explanation": "<one sentence>"}; parse
     defensively like `evals/judge.ts`. Map relevant->1, irrelevant->0.
   - Compare predicted label to the source's ground-truth `relevant`. Compute per-row and overall:
     accuracy, plus precision/recall on the "relevant" class. Print a per-source line and a summary.
   - Write `evals/apparatus-scores.json` (pretty JSON array; include per-source predicted label,
     ground truth, explanation). Set process.exitCode=1 only on hard failures (parse/network), NOT
     on a low accuracy number.

3) Add npm script to `package.json`: `"eval:apparatus": "tsx evals/run-apparatus.ts"` right after
   the `"eval:rubric"` entry if present, otherwise after `"eval:judge"`.

4) Append `evals/apparatus-scores.json` to `.gitignore`, matching the existing `evals/*.json` lines.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Do NOT import `src/utils/background-research.ts` — it pulls in browser-only deps (convex/browser,
  localStorage-bound bibliography) and will crash under Node. The query/context are provided inline
  in the dataset; you do not need to derive them.
- Do NOT modify `evals/run-experiment.ts`, `evals/judge.ts`, or `evals/run-rubric.ts`.
- Do NOT run the harness or anything needing network/secrets — no Bifrost key is available to you.
  Write the code only; it will be reviewed and run separately.
- TypeScript strict-clean (explicit types, no stray `any`), must pass the repo tsconfig and prettier.

content.text:
Two real problems in the rubric-judge dataset:
1. **`draftQuality` field is missing** — it's only in `case_id`, so the discrimination check (the key feature) pairs nothing.
2. **The "drafts" are written as persona *feedback/critique*** ("M. Le Stylo notes that…", "Professeur Athenæum observes…"), not as actual draft prose. `judgeDraft` scores a writer's draft, so these should be essay excerpts like `dataset.jsonl` uses — not commentary about a draft.

Since you want crush doing the work, I'll send it a focused correction. Let me write that spec.

input.content:
Fix ONLY the file `evals/rubric.jsonl` in the Twyne repo (cwd = repo root). Do not touch any other file. Two bugs to fix in the `rubric-judge` rows:

BUG 1 — missing field. Each `rubric-judge` row must carry an explicit
`"draftQuality": "strong"` or `"draftQuality": "weak"` field. Currently the quality is only
encoded in `case_id`, so `evals/run-rubric.ts` (which reads `row.draftQuality`) can't build the
strong-vs-weak discrimination pairs. Add the field to every rubric-judge row.

BUG 2 — wrong content. The `draftText` values are currently written as an EDITOR/PERSONA'S CRITIQUE
of a draft (e.g. "M. Le Stylo notes that the opening paragraph...", "Professeur Athenæum observes
that...", "Mlle. Sceptique finds..."). That is wrong: the production `judgeDraft` task scores the
WRITER'S OWN DRAFT PROSE, not feedback about it. Rewrite every rubric-judge `draftText` to be an
actual draft excerpt — real expository/argumentative prose a writer would submit — exactly like the
style in `evals/dataset.jsonl` (read it first to match register and length: 3-6 sentences,
first-or-third-person essay prose, NO persona names, NO "notes that"/"observes that" framing).

Keep the strong/weak contrast sharp so the judge can discriminate:
- `draftQuality: "strong"` → specific, concrete, well-argued prose: real claims, named specifics,
  earned transitions, no filler.
- `draftQuality: "weak"` → vague, generic, repetitive filler that could be about anything: empty
  praise, hedging, "in many ways", "a lot of strong moments", circular sentences, no specifics.

Keep the SAME `case_id`s and the SAME `persona` assignments and the SAME number of rows (3 strong +
3 weak across editor/scholar/devil), and keep the topic of each strong/weak pair roughly comparable
so the only real difference is quality. Leave the two `rubric-review` rows unchanged.

Output must remain valid JSONL (one compact JSON object per line). Do not run anything.

Two issues surfaced — one mechanical, one interesting:
- **504 timeouts** (editor_strong, scholar_strong): Bifrost's default 30s timeout; kimi is slow on longer prompts. This nulled 2 of 3 discrimination pairs. Needs retry.
- **faithful-to-grade 0/2**: both reviews flagged "invented" — either the review genuinely coins new numbers (a real finding) or the judge is miscalibrated. Let me inspect.

content.text: Confirmed — this is an **eval bug, not a model problem**. The reviews correctly cite the *supplied* per-judge scores (review_a's "6,5,4,8,9" exactly match editor=6/scholar=5/devil=4/angel=8/reader=9). The `faithful-to-grade` template omits the per-judge `judges[]` from its "GIVEN" block, so it wrongly flags faithful citations as "invented." Two fixes needed in `run-rubric.ts`: (1) include supplied per-judge scores in the faithfulness template; (2) retry on transient Bifrost 504s. Sending crush a focused fix.

input.content:
Fix TWO bugs in `evals/run-rubric.ts` in the Twyne repo (cwd = repo root). Change ONLY that file.
Read it first. Keep its style; keep tsc/prettier clean.

BUG 1 — false "invented" verdicts. `FAITHFUL_TO_GRADE_TEMPLATE` currently lists only combined / grade
/ judgeMean / staticTotal in its "GIVEN" block. It OMITS the per-judge scores (`judges[]`) and the
`staticFeedback[]` notes. As a result, when a review faithfully cites the SUPPLIED per-judge scores
(e.g. editor 6/10, devil 4/10), the judge has no record that those were given and wrongly labels the
review "invented". Verified against the data: review_a's cited 6,5,4,8,9 are exactly the supplied
editor=6/scholar=5/devil=4/angel=8/reader=9.
Fix: pass the supplied `judges` array and `staticFeedback` into the faithfulness check and render them
in the GIVEN block, e.g. a line per judge like "- editor: 6/10" and the static notes. Update the
template signature and its call site in `scoreRubricReviewRow` accordingly. The rule the judge applies
should become: "faithful" if the review only uses numbers among the supplied combined/grade/judgeMean/
staticTotal AND the per-judge scores listed; "invented" only if it states numeric scores NOT in that
supplied set, or contradicts the grade. Keep labels faithful=1 / invented=0.

BUG 2 — transient 504s null the discrimination pairs. The kimi judge model sometimes exceeds Bifrost's
default 30s gateway timeout and returns HTTP 504 (is_bifrost_error, request_timed_out). Two generations
(editor_strong, scholar_strong) failed this way, leaving null scores and FAILing otherwise-valid
discrimination pairs. Fix: add a small retry-with-backoff inside `callBifrost` (or a thin wrapper it
uses) — retry up to 3 attempts total on (a) network/abort errors and (b) HTTP 5xx / Bifrost 504
timeout responses, with a short backoff between attempts (e.g. 1.5s, then 4s). Do NOT retry on 4xx.
Keep the final error message if all attempts fail. Leave the per-attempt AbortSignal.timeout values as
they are (they're passed in by callers).

Do not change the dataset, other eval files, or anything outside `evals/run-rubric.ts`. Do not run
anything (no Bifrost key available to you).

Still 429/timeout even "solo" — the root cause is now clear: kimi-k2.6 on this gateway has a **2-concurrent-slot cap and is slow**, and an aborted slow request keeps running upstream (abort doesn't cancel it), so the retry collides with the abandoned slot. The discrimination logic is sound (editor 5>2, scholar 4>2 PASS); the failures are pure infra. 

Your newest message — *evaluate the evals themselves* (judge non-randomness, position bias, verbosity/content-amount bias, LLM-judge failure modes) — needs **many** judge calls, which kimi's limits will choke. So step one is picking a faster, higher-concurrency judge model. Let me probe what's reliable on the gateway.

input.command:
cd /home/diogenes/Projects/twyne
export SSL_CERT_FILE=/etc/pki/tls/certs/ca-bundle.crt NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS=/etc/pki/tls/certs/ca-bundle.crt CURL_CA_BUNDLE=/etc/pki/tls/certs/ca-bundle.crt
BASE="$(npx convex env get BIFROST_BASE_URL 2>/dev/null)"
KEY="$(npx convex env get BIFROST_API_KEY 2>/dev/null)"
probe () {
  local model="$1"
  local t0=$(date +%s.%N)
  local code=$(curl -s -o /tmp/probe_body -w "%{http_code}" --max-time 60 -X POST "$BASE/chat/completions" \
    -H "content-type: application/json" -H "x-bifrost-api-key: $KEY" \
    -d "{\"model\":\"$model\",\"messages\":[{\"role\":\"user\",\"content\":\"Reply with exactly: OK\"}],\"temperature\":0,\"max_tokens\":10}")
  local t1=$(date +%s.%N)
  printf "%-34s http=%s  %.1fs  %s\n" "$model" "$code" "$(echo "$t1-$t0"|bc)" "$(head -c 120 /tmp/probe_body | tr -d '\n')"
}
probe "neuralwatt/qwen3.5-397b-fast"
probe "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6"
probe "neuralwatt/kimi-k2.6"

1. **faithfulness judge over-strict (different bug now):** The per-judge-score fix worked, but the judge now flags *non-numeric* observations ("three uncited claims", counts of "studies show") as "invented." Those are legitimate — the reviewer is *given the draft* and can cite it. The check should be **numeric-only** (fabricated/contradictory scores or grades), ignoring textual observations.
2. **devil_strong is still critique-voice, not a draft:** crush fixed editor/scholar but left `devil_strong` written as criticism of an essay ("…the writer has not earned it"), while `devil_weak` is a primary essay. That's a *genre* mismatch confounding the strong-vs-weak contrast → both scored 3.

Problem: its `draftText` is written as a CRITIC's commentary about someone else's essay

essay on its back, and the writer has not earned it. Fama's 1970..."). That is the wrong genre — the
production `judgeDraft` task scores a WRITER'S OWN PRIMARY DRAFT, not a critique of one. Its paired
`rj_devil_weak` is a primary essay about market efficiency, so the strong/weak pair is currently
comparing two different genres instead of two qualities.
Rewrite `rj_devil_strong`'s `draftText` as a STRONG PRIMARY ESSAY DRAFT on the SAME topic as
rj_devil_weak (market efficiency) — first/third-person expository prose that TAKES A CLEAR POSITION
and argues it with real specifics (e.g. distinguishes Fama's 1970 efficient-market claim from
Shiller's volatility critique, names the price-reflects-value vs price-reflects-information
equivocation and resolves it). NO "the writer", NO "the essay", NO "that sentence" meta-commentary —

hedgy filler in rj_devil_weak. Keep the same case_id, persona ("devil"), task, and
draftQuality:"strong". Change nothing else in the file.

"invented" for stating NON-numeric specifics it legitimately drew from the draft (the review IS given
the full draft via buildRubricReviewPrompt), e.g. "three uncited claims", counting how often
"studies show" appears, naming repeated phrases. Those are grounded observations, not fabrications.
Narrow the check to NUMERIC consistency ONLY:
  - Make the template state that the reviewer was ALSO given the full draft, so specific NON-numeric
    observations about the draft's wording, claims, structure, or word counts are EXPECTED and must
    NOT be treated as "invented".
  - Decision rule: answer "faithful" if every NUMERIC SCORE or LETTER GRADE the review states is
    among the supplied figures (the combined /100, the letter grade, judgeMean /10, staticTotal /10,
    or any of the per-judge X/10 scores) AND it does not contradict the given grade. Answer
    "invented" ONLY if the review states a numeric score or grade that is NOT in that supplied set, or
    contradicts the given grade. Non-numeric specifics never make it "invented".
Keep labels faithful=1 / invented=0, keep the signature that already receives judges[] and
staticFeedback, keep the GIVEN block listing the per-judge scores and static notes.

input.description: Verify devil_strong rewrite and typecheck

content.content:
=== devil_strong now ===
Markets are not efficient in any single, settled sense, and pretending otherwise is what makes most essays on the subject incoherent. Fama's 1970 paper argued that prices reflect available information under strong assumptions about rational investors and independent errors; Shiller's 1981 and 2000 work showed that the volatility of equities, bonds, and housing prices systematically violates those assumptions, and the dividend-price ratio predicts multi-year returns better than any efficient-market story would allow. The equivocation in the standard textbook version — 'price reflects information' said as if it implied 'price reflects value' — collapses the moment you separate the two: informational efficiency is a statistical claim about signal-extraction, valuation is a claim about the mapping from cash flows to present value, and nothing in Fama's framework licenses the second from the first. The defensible position is the weak one: prices are the best forecast of value we have, not the same thing as value, and anyone who treats the market as a metronome for worth is going to be both surprised and instructive when it isn't. The piece should commit to that weak claim, name the Fama-Shiller dispute instead of gesturing at 'the literature,' and stop borrowing the strong version's rhetorical force while only defending the weak one.
=== typecheck/prettier ===
tsc 0
All matched files use Prettier code style!

content.text: Now let me write the spec for the judge-robustness meta-eval suite (covers your "mutation/amputation/similarity" + "reliability/position-bias/verbosity-bias/failure-modes" asks). This is the substantive piece.

input.content:
Add a JUDGE-ROBUSTNESS meta-eval to the Twyne repo (cwd = repo root). This does NOT test Twyne's
outputs — it stress-tests the LLM-as-judge itself for known failure modes. Match the style of the
existing eval harness.

STUDY FIRST (read, do not modify): `evals/judge.ts` and `evals/run-rubric.ts`. REUSE from them:
- The header-only Bifrost auth (ONLY header is `x-bifrost-api-key`; NEVER an Authorization/Bearer —
  Bifrost 401s on a bearer).
- The retry-with-backoff Bifrost caller in `run-rubric.ts` (retries on network/abort and on
  5xx/504/408 AND 429 concurrent_budget_exceeded — IMPORTANT: this suite makes ~60 sequential calls,
  so you MUST also retry HTTP 429 with backoff; if run-rubric's retry doesn't cover 429, add 429 to
  the retryable statuses in YOUR copy). All calls are SEQUENTIAL (never fire concurrent requests — the
  gateway caps concurrency).
- Defensive JSON parsing of judge replies (strip ``` fences, extract first {...}).
- Env: `BIFROST_BASE_URL`, `BIFROST_API_KEY`, and `JUDGE_MODEL` (DEFAULT
  "neuralwatt/qwen3.5-397b-fast" — NOT kimi; kimi is slow + 2-slot capped). Optional `JUDGE_MODEL_2`
  for the cross-model probe (skip that probe if unset).

The judge under test is the "grounded vs generic" rubric used in `evals/judge.ts`
(faithfulness-to-draft): given a DRAFT and a FEEDBACK, answer "grounded" (feedback engages THIS
draft's specifics) or "generic" (boilerplate that fits any text). Map grounded=1, generic=0. Use this
same rubric wording for all probes so results are comparable.

CREATE:
1) `evals/run-robustness.ts` with hand-authored controlled FIXTURES (inline typed constants are fine)
   and the following probes. Each probe prints a clear `[PASS]`/`[FAIL]` line plus its metric, and
   contributes its raw verdicts to `evals/robustness-scores.json`.

   PROBE 1 — RELIABILITY / non-randomness (test-retest):
     Take >=4 CLEAR cases (2 truly grounded feedbacks, 2 truly generic) plus 1-2 BORDERLINE cases.
     Call the judge N=5 times each at temperature 0. For each case compute the modal label and the
     agreement fraction (modal_count/5). PASS if every CLEAR case is 5/5 self-consistent AND its modal
     label equals the expected label. Borderline cases are REPORT-ONLY (print their split, don't fail).
     Report overall flip-rate.

   PROBE 2 — POSITION / ORDERING bias:
     (a) field-order: run the SAME clear cases with the prompt rendering DRAFT-then-FEEDBACK, and again
         with FEEDBACK-then-DRAFT (same instruction). PASS if the label is identical across the two
         orderings for every case.
     (b) pairwise slot bias: for >=3 (draft, A=grounded feedback, B=generic feedback) triples, ask the
         judge which feedback better engages the draft, presented as (A then B) and again as (B then
         A). A slot-biased judge keeps picking the same POSITION. PASS if it picks the same CONTENT (A)
         in BOTH orderings for every triple. Report the slot-consistency vs content-consistency rates.

   PROBE 3 — VERBOSITY / content-amount bias:
     Controlled set decoupling length from quality:
       - concise_grounded (short, specific) -> expect grounded
       - verbose_generic (long filler, no specifics) -> expect generic
       - padded_grounded (= concise_grounded + a big block of irrelevant filler appended) -> expect
         grounded (unchanged)
     PASS if the judge labels match expectations for ALL three — i.e. it does NOT reward length:
     verbose_generic stays generic, and padding does not flip grounded. Report any length-driven flip.

   PROBE 4 — MUTATION (sensitivity vs robustness):
     Start from a grounded feedback F about draft D.
       - meaning-changing mutation: rewrite F to remove every draft-specific reference (replace
         specifics with generic phrasings) -> expect flip to GENERIC (sensitivity: caught).
       - cosmetic mutation: paraphrase / reorder F's sentences but keep all specifics -> expect label
         UNCHANGED = grounded (robustness: ignored cosmetic change).
     PASS if BOTH hold (sensitive to meaning, robust to cosmetics).

   PROBE 5 — AMPUTATION (does the judge actually use the DRAFT input?):
     Take a grounded (D, F) pair. Build a variant that keeps F but swaps D for an UNRELATED draft D2
     (and a second variant with an empty draft). A judge that truly conditions on the draft must now
     answer GENERIC (F no longer matches the shown draft). PASS if the verdict flips to generic when
     the draft is swapped/emptied. If it stays grounded, the judge is ignoring the draft and
     rubber-stamping the feedback — FAIL. This is the most important probe; print it prominently.

   PROBE 6 — CONFIDENT-BULLSHIT hard negative:
     A feedback that is polished and specific-SOUNDING but whose specifics are fabricated / about a
     DIFFERENT topic than the draft -> expect generic (judge not fooled by surface polish/confidence).
     PASS if generic. Report-only contribution to a "hardness" note.

   PROBE 7 — CROSS-MODEL agreement (optional; skip cleanly if JUDGE_MODEL_2 unset):
     Re-run PROBE 1's clear cases under JUDGE_MODEL_2 and report the label-agreement rate vs
     JUDGE_MODEL. Report-only (low agreement = judge is model-dependent).

2) Write `evals/robustness-scores.json` — a structured object with each probe's raw verdicts + metrics.
3) Print a final SUMMARY block: one line per probe with PASS/FAIL and its key number, then an overall
   verdict. Set `process.exitCode = 1` if any HARD failure occurs (network/parse after retries) OR if
   any of PROBES 1,2,3,4,5,6 FAIL. Probe 7 and borderline cases never set the exit code.
4) Add npm script `"eval:robustness": "tsx evals/run-robustness.ts"` right after `"eval:apparatus"`.
5) Append `evals/robustness-scores.json` to `.gitignore`.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Do NOT modify any other eval file.
- Do NOT run the harness (no Bifrost key available to you). Write code only.
- All judge calls SEQUENTIAL with the retry-enabled caller (incl. 429). temperature 0 except the
  reliability probe which is explicitly temp 0 too (we are testing determinism), and you may add ONE
  optional temp-0.7 reliability variant on the borderline cases (report-only) to illustrate spread.
- TypeScript strict-clean, must pass repo tsconfig + prettier. Explicit types, no stray `any`.
- Author fixtures so each expected label is genuinely unambiguous for the CLEAR cases; make the
  borderline and confident-bullshit cases actually hard (that is the point of the suite).

content.text:
Got it — important clarification: **mutation and amputation testing are for the application as a whole**, not just the LLM-judge. Those are distinct from the judge-robustness suite (which covers your separate "judge reliability/bias" ask). 

- **Mutation testing** = inject faults into the source and check the test suite catches them (test-suite quality).
- **Amputation testing** = disable a component/provider and verify graceful degradation (Twyne has many fallback paths: Bifrost→local, TinyFish→local stub, Arize/PostHog dormant, Convex-absent no-op).

Let me survey the existing test suite to ground that work while the robustness build finishes.

input.command:
cd /home/diogenes/Projects/twyne
echo "=== test files ==="; find . -path ./node_modules -prune -o \( -name "*.test.ts" -o -name "*.spec.ts" -o -name "*.test.tsx" \) -print 2>/dev/null | grep -v node_modules
echo "=== tests/ dir ==="; ls -la tests/ 2>/dev/null
echo "=== test runner / coverage deps ==="; python3 -c "import json;p=json.load(open('package.json'));d={**p.get('devDependencies',{}),**p.get('dependencies',{})};print('\n'.join(f'{k}={v}' for k,v in d.items() if any(x in k.lower() for x in ['stryker','vitest','jest','playwright','test','coverage','c8','nyc'])))"
echo "=== fallback/dormant paths (amputation targets) ==="; grep -rnE "process.env.(BIFROST|TINYFISH|ARIZE|POSTHOG|RESEND)" convex/*.ts | grep -iE "if|\?\?|!|return" | head -20

content.content:
TypeScript/JavaScript code similarity analyzer

Usage: similarity-ts [OPTIONS] [PATHS]...

Arguments:
  [PATHS]...  Paths to analyze (files or directories) [default: .]

Options:
  -p, --print
          Print code in output
  -t, --threshold <THRESHOLD>
          Similarity threshold (0.0-1.0) [default: 0.87]
      --no-functions
          Disable function similarity checking
      --types
          Enable type similarity checking (includes type literals by default)
      --no-types
          Disable type similarity checking
      --classes
          Enable class similarity checking
      --classes-only
          Only check classes (exclude functions and types)
      --include-inheritance
          Include classes with inheritance (extends) - excluded by default
      --include-implements
          Include classes with interface implementation (implements) - excluded by default
      --suggest
          Show refactoring suggestions for excluded classes
  -e, --extensions <EXTENSIONS>
          File extensions to check
  -m, --min-lines <MIN_LINES>
          Minimum lines for functions to be considered [default: 3]
      --min-tokens <MIN_TOKENS>
          Minimum tokens for functions to be considered
  -r, --rename-cost <RENAME_COST>
          Rename cost for APTED algorithm [default: 0.3]
      --no-size-penalty
          Disable size penalty for very different sized functions
      --filter-function <FILTER_FUNCTION>
          Filter functions by name (substring match)

content.text:
The robustness suite found **real LLM-judge failure modes** — exactly what you asked for:

- **PROBE 1 reliability: PASS** — 4/4 clear cases 5/5 deterministic, 0 flips (judge isn't random at temp 0, even at 0.7).
- **PROBE 3 verbosity: PASS** — length doesn't drive verdicts.
- **PROBE 6 confident-bullshit: PASS** — not fooled by polished fabrication.
- **PROBE 2 position bias: FAIL** — on the *close* pair (ramen), the judge picked whichever feedback was in the first slot (AB→a, BA→b). Classic slot bias on borderline pairs.
- **PROBE 4 mutation: FAIL** — stripping draft-specifics didn't flip grounded→generic.
- **PROBE 5 amputation: FAIL (most important)** — with an *empty* draft the judge still said "grounded," i.e. it rubber-stamps the feedback's own specificity instead of cross-checking the draft. (It *did* correctly catch an *unrelated* draft.)

Let me verify probes 4 and 5 aren't fixture artifacts before reporting them as judge weaknesses.

  `work, otherwise it reads as cherry-picking.`;

/** Meaning-changing mutation: keep the surface, gut every draft-specific
 *  reference. Should flip to generic. */
const F_MUTATED_MEANING =
  `The architectural comparison is the spine of the piece, and the specific ` +
  `point about one building's reception of the other's ideas is exactly what ` +
  `makes the argument original. I'd push back on the claim that two examples ` +
  `"refute" uniformity — at minimum you need more examples from other ` +
  `architects of the era, otherwise it reads as cherry-picking.`;

/** Cosmetic mutation: paraphrase + reorder, keep every specific. Should
 *  remain grounded. */
const F_MUTATED_COSMETIC =
  `While the comparison between FAU-USP and CECIERJ carries the essay, it's ` +
  `the precise observation that the compressed pilotis undermined the social ` +
  `flow diagram that gives the argument its originality. However, asserting ` +
  `that just two buildings "refute" uniformity invites a cherry-picking ` +
  `objection — adding a counter-example from Lina Bo Bardi, or from Vilanova ` +
  `Artigas's later work, would close that gap.`;

/** Padding block — irrelevant filler about cooking. */
const PADDING_BLOCK =
  `\n\nA note on risotto: the rice should be arborio or carnaroli, never ` +
  `long-grain. Toast the grains in butter for two minutes before adding any ` +
  `liquid, and add stock one ladle at a time, stirring constantly. Wine goes ` +
  `in after the toast, before the first ladle. Finish off-heat with cold ` +
  `butter and Parmigiano-Reggiano — never Parmesan with a generic label. ` +
  `Rest the pan for sixty seconds before plating. A well-made risotto should ` +
  `flow slowly when you tap the plate, an Italian cooks' shorthand for ` +
  `"all'onda", meaning "like a wave". This is unrelated to the essay above; ` +
  `it is included here purely as filler to test whether the judge rewards ` +
  `length.`;

The suite is working: qwen-as-judge is *reliable and verbosity/bullshit-resistant*, but has *position bias on close pairs*, *weak sensitivity to specifics-stripping*, and *rubber-stamps feedback against an empty draft*. content.text: While Stryker runs, let me record this substantial eval/testing work to memory.

input.content:
---
name: twyne-eval-testing-harness
description: "Twyne evals/ harness + mutation/similarity tooling — npm scripts, judge model, gotchas"
metadata:
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: 6e1b765d-5eab-471f-ac93-97d36f0ec721
---

Twyne has an offline eval + testing harness under `evals/` plus mutation/duplication tooling. All eval scripts run via `tsx`, reuse the REAL prompt builders (`convex/agentPrompts.ts`) + `PERSONAS` (`src/utils/personas.ts`), and call the Bifrost gateway with **header-only auth** (`x-bifrost-api-key`; NEVER an Authorization/Bearer — Bifrost 401s on a bearer). Secrets read at runtime via `npx convex env get BIFROST_BASE_URL|BIFROST_API_KEY`. See [[arize-evals-integration]].

**Judge model: use `neuralwatt/qwen3.5-397b-fast` (fast ~1s, available), NOT `neuralwatt/kimi-k2.6`.** kimi has a 2-concurrent-slot cap + is slow → 504 gateway timeouts and 429 `concurrent_budget_exceeded`. NEVER run two eval scripts at once (the 2-slot cap starves both). `anthropic/*` on the gateway is out of credits (400 "credit balance too low"). Set `JUDGE_MODEL=neuralwatt/qwen3.5-397b-fast` for every eval run.

**npm scripts:**
- `eval` → `evals/run-experiment.ts`: persona feedback/rewrite/riff generations → `evals/runs.json`.
- `eval:judge` → `evals/judge.ts`: scores runs.json with faithfulness-to-draft + feedback-helpfulness rubrics → `evals/scores.json`. Verified 7/7 + negative control discriminates.
- `eval:rubric` → `evals/run-rubric.ts`: replays production `judgeDraft` (JSON {score,rationale}, JUDGE TASK suffix copied verbatim from agents.ts) + `reviewRubric` narrative. Scores score-in-range, rationale-grounded, faithful-to-grade, has-revision-plan, and strong>weak discrimination pairs. Dataset `evals/rubric.jsonl` (6 rubric-judge rows w/ `draftQuality` strong|weak across editor/scholar/devil + 2 rubric-review rows). GREEN: 6/6 in-range, 6/6 grounded, 2/2 faithful, 2/2 plan, 3/3 discrimination. Gotcha fixed: faithfulness judge must receive the supplied per-judge `judges[]` scores in its GIVEN block and only flag NUMERIC fabrications (non-numeric draft observations are legit — the reviewer is given the draft).
- `eval:apparatus` → `evals/run-apparatus.ts`: source-RELEVANCE judge over canned `candidateSources` (ground-truth `relevant` bool) in `evals/apparatus.jsonl`. The apparatus search itself is NOT an LLM call (`deriveQuery` is string concat; intelligence is the provider returning relevant Sources). Do NOT import `src/utils/background-research.ts` (browser deps). GREEN: 20/20 acc/P/R.
- `eval:robustness` → `evals/run-robustness.ts`: meta-eval of the LLM-as-judge itself (7 probes, 429-aware retry, sequential). FINDINGS on qwen judge: reliability PASS (0 flips temp 0 & 0.7), verbosity PASS, confident-bullshit PASS; but **position bias on close pairs** (slot-picks), **weak mutation sensitivity** (specifics-stripping didn't flip grounded→generic), and **amputation FAIL — empty draft still scored "grounded"** (judge keys off the feedback's own specificity, doesn't truly cross-check the draft). These are real qwen-judge weaknesses, not fixture artifacts. Set `JUDGE_MODEL_2` to enable cross-model agreement probe.
- `test:mutation` → `stryker run` (Stryker 9.6.1, devDep). Config `stryker.config.json`.

All `evals/*-scores.json` + runs.json are gitignored.

**Mutation testing (Stryker) gotchas:** No native `bun:test` runner → use the **command runner** (`testRunner:"command"`, `commandRunner.command:"bun test <files>"`), `coverageAnalysis:"off"`, `checkers:[]`. Baseline test command MUST be green. Scope `mutate` to source files with 1:1 bun tests (currently `src/utils/{reasoning-tags,feature-flags,auth-preference}.ts`; note provider-presets/ui-defaults/persona-voices have tests but no same-named source). **Critical:** the repo root has many agent dot-dirs (`.claude .crush .factory .pi .codex .cursor .gemini .opencode .agents .zero ...`) whose `skills/arize-*` are SYMLINKED dirs that crash Stryker's sandbox copy with `EISDIR`; they must all be in `ignorePatterns` (Stryker did not honor .gitignore for them).

**Duplication: `similarity-ts` CLI** (`~/.cargo/bin/similarity-ts`, run `similarity-ts src convex evals`). Real dups it found: `extractTaggedJson` is 100% identical in `convex/agents.ts` ↔ `src/utils/ai-client.ts` (also `parseJudgeOutput` 93%, `normalizeInterviewDossierDraft` 93% — server/client copies that can drift); eval `callBifrost` ~97% dup across harnesses; `BlogPostData` interface defined twice; several `timeAgo`/`getTimeAgo` copies.

Eval implementation was done by the **crush** CLI (`crush run --quiet "<spec>"`, no --yolo); each spec tightly scoped per file, reviewed + run by me (crush never gets the Bifrost key — I run the harnesses).

**Judge-robustness findings (your "is the judge biased / random / failure modes" ask):** qwen-as-judge is *reliable* (0 flips at temp 0 and 0.7), *not verbosity-biased*, *not fooled by confident-bullshit* — **but** it has **position bias on close pairs**, **weak sensitivity** to specifics-stripping, and **rubber-stamps an empty draft as "grounded"** (it keys off the feedback's own specificity instead of truly cross-checking the draft). Real, documented weaknesses.

**Key infra note:** I switched the eval judge model to `neuralwatt/qwen3.5-397b-fast` — `kimi-k2.6` has a 2-slot concurrency cap and times out; never run two evals at once.

These are each substantial and divergent, so I'd rather you pick the order than guess:

2. **Twyne MCP server** — expose Twyne (personas/feedback/rubric/apparatus) over MCP for other apps/agents. Largest item.
3. **Stream comment/persona responses** in the UI.
4. **Persona replies respond to your in-text replies.**

10	In Twyne, replying to an editor's **inline persona note** (the manuscript modal opened by clicking a `.twyne-persona-note`) works via a window CustomEvent, not a direct call. The modal lives in `src/components/editor/twyne-editor.tsx`; the reply thread + AI persona response logic (`submitReply(noteId, askPersona)`) lives in `src/components/personas/personas-panel.tsx`.
11	
12	- Editor modal dispatches `twyne:persona-reply` `{ noteId, text }` and closes itself.
13	- `PersonasPanel` listens (useVisibleTask$ + cleanup), sets `store.replyDraft`, calls `submitReply(noteId, true)` → files the writer's reply, then pulls the editor back in (BYOK client AI, Convex `api.agents.runPersona` fallback, then canned fallback).
14	- The editor **route** (`src/routes/editor/index.tsx`) also listens and reveals the Cast panel (`store.rightPanel="personas"; store.rightPanelOpen=true`).
15	
16	Gotcha that was broken after the crush restore: this event had **no listener** — replies silently did nothing. Also the right-board panels were conditionally mounted (`store.rightPanel === X && <Panel/>`), so a panel's listeners were dead unless its tab was active. Fixed by keeping all four panels always-mounted and toggling visibility with a `hidden` class wrapper (also preserves tab state). Related: [[entire-checkpoint-recovery]].

file.content:
---
name: persona-reply-event-wiring
description: "Twyne inline-note reply flow: editor modal dispatches twyne:persona-reply, PersonasPanel consumes it"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: c3483273-e244-4fc5-afea-8a45129456f4
---

In Twyne, replying to an editor's **inline persona note** (the manuscript modal opened by clicking a `.twyne-persona-note`) works via a window CustomEvent, not a direct call. The modal lives in `src/components/editor/twyne-editor.tsx`; the reply thread + AI persona response logic (`submitReply(noteId, askPersona)`) lives in `src/components/personas/personas-panel.tsx`.

- Editor modal dispatches `twyne:persona-reply` `{ noteId, text }` and closes itself.
- `PersonasPanel` listens (useVisibleTask$ + cleanup), sets `store.replyDraft`, calls `submitReply(noteId, true)` → files the writer's reply, then pulls the editor back in (BYOK client AI, Convex `api.agents.runPersona` fallback, then canned fallback).
- The editor **route** (`src/routes/editor/index.tsx`) also listens and reveals the Cast panel (`store.rightPanel="personas"; store.rightPanelOpen=true`).

Gotcha that was broken after the crush restore: this event had **no listener** — replies silently did nothing. Also the right-board panels were conditionally mounted (`store.rightPanel === X && <Panel/>`), so a panel's listeners were dead unless its tab was active. Fixed by keeping all four panels always-mounted and toggling visibility with a `hidden` class wrapper (also preserves tab state). Related: [[entire-checkpoint-recovery]].

content.text: Confirmed the core gap: the editor's `repliesByNote` (lines 91, 1162) is for **user comments**, not persona replies. The persona reply *backend exists and works* (client `persona-reply` at ai-client.ts:250; Convex `runPersona` with `instruction:"elaborate"`), but the answer lands only in the Cast side-panel — never back in the text. Let me confirm what the inline note popover currently renders.

Confirmed: the inline popover shows the note + a reply box, but **never renders the persona's response thread** — after replying it closes, and the answer lives only in the Cast side-panel. The reply *generation* works (client `persona-reply` + Convex `runPersona` both accept `priorMessages`/`userMessage` for multi-turn); the gap is surfacing it back in the text. 

One genuine UX decision shapes the implementation:

questions.question: When you reply to a persona's note in the text, how should the persona's response appear?

options.description: Keep the note popover open as a conversation: your reply + a 'typing…' indicator + the persona's response appear right there, and you can continue the back-and-forth without leaving the text. (Also still syncs to the Cast panel.)

input.content:
# Persona reply responsiveness — live inline thread in the text

## Context

When the writer replies to an editor's **inline persona note** (the popover opened by
clicking a `.twyne-persona-note` span in the manuscript), the persona's AI answer is
generated correctly but only ever appears in the Cast **side-panel** thread — never back
in the text. The inline popover closes on send, so from the writer's point of view "in
the text" nothing happens. The user wants the model to respond **in the text**: the note
popover should become a live conversation (writer reply → typing indicator → persona
response → continue the back-and-forth) without leaving the editor.

The backend already supports this — `submitReply(noteId, askPersona)` in
`personas-panel.tsx` files the reply and calls client AI (`runClientAgent("persona-reply", …)`,
`src/utils/ai-client.ts:250`) or Convex `api.agents.runPersona` (`convex/agents.ts:597`,
`instruction:"elaborate"`), both of which accept `priorMessages` + `userMessage` for
multi-turn. The only gap is **surfacing the thread back into the editor popover**. The
editor and panel are separate components that already communicate over a `twyne:*` window
`CustomEvent` bus, so we extend that bus rather than add shared state.

## Approach

Keep `submitReply` as the single source of truth for filing replies + generating
responses. Add events so the panel streams thread state to the editor, and make the
editor popover render the thread + typing indicator and stay open.

### Event contract (new `twyne:*` events)
- Panel → editor `twyne:persona-reply-thread` `{ noteId, replies: PersonaReply[] }` — full
  current thread; emitted after the optimistic user-reply add and again after the persona
  response lands.
- Panel → editor `twyne:persona-replying` `{ noteId, replying: boolean }` — toggles the
  typing indicator around the AI call.
- Panel → editor `twyne:persona-reply-error` `{ noteId, message }` — surfaces the existing
  `store.conveneError` cases inline (today they fail silently from the popover's view).
- Editor → panel `twyne:request-persona-thread` `{ noteId }` — fired when a popover opens so
  prior history renders immediately.

Reuse the shared `PersonaReply` type from `src/types/index.ts` (already exported; imported
elsewhere via `src/utils/convex-sync.ts`).

### `src/components/personas/personas-panel.tsx`
- Add a small `$()` helper `emitReplyThread(noteId)` that dispatches
  `twyne:persona-reply-thread` with `store.repliesByNote[noteId] ?? []`.
- In `submitReply` (line ~763): after the optimistic user reply is pushed into
  `store.repliesByNote` (~786) call `emitReplyThread(noteId)`; when `askPersona`, dispatch
  `twyne:persona-replying {replying:true}` before the AI call and `{replying:false}` in a
  `finally`; after the persona reply is appended (~927) call `emitReplyThread(noteId)`; on
  each existing error/early-return branch dispatch `twyne:persona-reply-error` with the same
  message currently put in `store.conveneError`.
- In the existing `useVisibleTask$` that listens for `twyne:persona-reply` (~958), also add a
  `twyne:request-persona-thread` listener that replies with `emitReplyThread(detail.noteId)`.
  (The current `onReply` handler that calls `submitReply(detail.noteId, true)` is unchanged.)

### `src/components/editor/twyne-editor.tsx`
- Extend the `notePopover` store shape with `thread: PersonaReply[]`, `replying: boolean`,
  and `error: string | null` (import `PersonaReply` from `~/types`).
- Where the popover is populated from the clicked span's `data-persona-note-*` attributes
  (~577): initialize `thread: []`, `replying: false`, `error: null`, and dispatch
  `twyne:request-persona-thread {noteId: id}`.
- In the popover's existing `useVisibleTask$` listener block (~808, alongside
  `twyne:persona-notes`): add listeners for `twyne:persona-reply-thread`,
  `twyne:persona-replying`, `twyne:persona-reply-error` that update `store.notePopover` only
  when `detail.noteId === store.notePopover?.id`.
- Render the thread inside the popover body between the note text (~2363) and the reply
  textarea (~2377): map `thread` to chat bubbles — `authorKind:"user"` right-aligned in a
  neutral paper tone, `authorKind:"persona"` left-aligned with `notePopover.color`; show a
  `typing…` bubble when `replying`; show `error` in a small warning line when set.
- On reply submit (the ⌘↩ handler ~2392 and the Reply button ~2429): **do not** set
  `store.notePopover = null`. Instead clear only the popover `draft`, optimistically nothing
  else (the panel emits the thread), and keep the popover pinned/open. Dispatch
  `twyne:persona-reply` exactly as today. Keep "Strike" (dismiss) closing the popover.
- Make the popover sticky during a conversation: when `replying` or `thread.length > 0`,
  treat it like `pinned` so the existing `onMouseLeave$`/`onMouseLeave` auto-close (~2303,
  ~660) does not dismiss it mid-thread.

### Route (`src/routes/editor/index.tsx`)
- No change required; it already reveals the Cast panel on `twyne:persona-reply`. The
  inline thread and the Cast-panel thread stay in sync because both read the same
  `repliesByNote` / Convex `api.sync.addPersonaReply` writes that `submitReply` already does.

## Reuse
- `submitReply`, `runClientAgent("persona-reply")`, `api.agents.runPersona` — unchanged.
- Window `CustomEvent` bus pattern — same as `twyne:persona-reply` / `twyne:persona-notes`.
- `PersonaReply` type — `src/types/index.ts:186`.
- Existing popover styling tokens (`--color-paper*`, `notePopover.color`).

## Verification
1. `npm run dev`, open the editor, generate persona feedback so inline notes appear.
2. Click a `.twyne-persona-note`, type a reply, ⌘↩: popover stays open, your reply appears
   as a bubble, a `typing…` indicator shows, then the persona's response appears inline.
   Send a second reply → multi-turn continues in place.
3. Open the Cast panel → the same thread is mirrored (sync intact).
4. Failure surfacing: with no provider configured and signed out, replying shows the inline
   error instead of doing nothing.
5. Regression: clicking a note without replying still auto-closes on mouse-leave; "Strike"
   still dismisses; `npm run lint` + `bun test src/components/editor` pass.

## Out of scope
Streaming tokens for the reply (separate request), changing the Cast-panel thread UI, and
making non-persona (user) comments conversational.

toolUseResult.content:
# Persona reply responsiveness — live inline thread in the text

## Context

When the writer replies to an editor's **inline persona note** (the popover opened by
clicking a `.twyne-persona-note` span in the manuscript), the persona's AI answer is
generated correctly but only ever appears in the Cast **side-panel** thread — never back
in the text. The inline popover closes on send, so from the writer's point of view "in
the text" nothing happens. The user wants the model to respond **in the text**: the note
popover should become a live conversation (writer reply → typing indicator → persona
response → continue the back-and-forth) without leaving the editor.

The backend already supports this — `submitReply(noteId, askPersona)` in
`personas-panel.tsx` files the reply and calls client AI (`runClientAgent("persona-reply", …)`,
`src/utils/ai-client.ts:250`) or Convex `api.agents.runPersona` (`convex/agents.ts:597`,
`instruction:"elaborate"`), both of which accept `priorMessages` + `userMessage` for
multi-turn. The only gap is **surfacing the thread back into the editor popover**. The
editor and panel are separate components that already communicate over a `twyne:*` window
`CustomEvent` bus, so we extend that bus rather than add shared state.

## Approach

Keep `submitReply` as the single source of truth for filing replies + generating
responses. Add events so the panel streams thread state to the editor, and make the
editor popover render the thread + typing indicator and stay open.

### Event contract (new `twyne:*` events)
- Panel → editor `twyne:persona-reply-thread` `{ noteId, replies: PersonaReply[] }` — full
  current thread; emitted after the optimistic user-reply add and again after the persona
  response lands.
- Panel → editor `twyne:persona-replying` `{ noteId, replying: boolean }` — toggles the
  typing indicator around the AI call.
- Panel → editor `twyne:persona-reply-error` `{ noteId, message }` — surfaces the existing
  `store.conveneError` cases inline (today they fail silently from the popover's view).
- Editor → panel `twyne:request-persona-thread` `{ noteId }` — fired when a popover opens so
  prior history renders immediately.

Reuse the shared `PersonaReply` type from `src/types/index.ts:186` (already exported; used
via `src/utils/convex-sync.ts`).

### `src/components/personas/personas-panel.tsx`
- Add a small `$()` helper `emitReplyThread(noteId)` that dispatches
  `twyne:persona-reply-thread` with `store.repliesByNote[noteId] ?? []`.
- In `submitReply` (line ~763): after the optimistic user reply is pushed into
  `store.repliesByNote` (~786) call `emitReplyThread(noteId)`; when `askPersona`, dispatch
  `twyne:persona-replying {replying:true}` before the AI call and `{replying:false}` in the
  `finally` (~947); after the persona reply is appended (~927) call `emitReplyThread(noteId)`;
  on each existing error/early-return branch dispatch `twyne:persona-reply-error` with the
  same message currently assigned to `store.conveneError`.
- In the existing `useVisibleTask$` that listens for `twyne:persona-reply` (~958), add a
  `twyne:request-persona-thread` listener that calls `emitReplyThread(detail.noteId)`. The
  current `onReply` handler (`submitReply(detail.noteId, true)`) is unchanged.

### `src/components/editor/twyne-editor.tsx`
- Extend the `notePopover` store shape with `thread: PersonaReply[]`, `replying: boolean`,
  and `error: string | null` (import `PersonaReply` from `~/types`).
- Where the popover is populated from the clicked span's `data-persona-note-*` attributes
  (~577): initialize `thread: []`, `replying: false`, `error: null`, and dispatch
  `twyne:request-persona-thread {noteId: id}`.
- In the popover's existing `useVisibleTask$` listener block (~808, alongside
  `twyne:persona-notes`): add listeners for `twyne:persona-reply-thread`,
  `twyne:persona-replying`, `twyne:persona-reply-error` that update `store.notePopover` only
  when `detail.noteId === store.notePopover?.id`.
- Render the thread inside the popover body between the note text (~2363) and the reply
  textarea (~2377): map `thread` to chat bubbles — `authorKind:"user"` right-aligned in a
  neutral paper tone, `authorKind:"persona"` left-aligned tinted with `notePopover.color`;
  show a `typing…` bubble when `replying`; show `error` in a small warning line when set.
- On reply submit (the ⌘↩ handler ~2392 and the Reply button ~2429): **do not** set
  `store.notePopover = null`. Clear only the popover `draft` and keep the popover open.
  Dispatch `twyne:persona-reply` exactly as today; the thread updates via events. Keep
  "Strike" (dismiss) closing the popover.
- Keep the popover sticky during a conversation: when `replying` or `thread.length > 0`,
  treat it like `pinned` so the existing `onMouseLeave$` auto-close (~2303, ~660) does not
  dismiss it mid-thread.

### Route (`src/routes/editor/index.tsx`)
- No change required; it already reveals the Cast panel on `twyne:persona-reply`. Inline and
  Cast-panel threads stay in sync because both read the same `repliesByNote` / Convex
  `api.sync.addPersonaReply` writes `submitReply` already performs.

## Reuse
- `submitReply`, `runClientAgent("persona-reply")`, `api.agents.runPersona` — unchanged.
- Window `CustomEvent` bus pattern — same as `twyne:persona-reply` / `twyne:persona-notes`.
- `PersonaReply` type — `src/types/index.ts:186`.
- Existing popover styling tokens (`--color-paper*`, `notePopover.color`).

## Verification
1. `npm run dev`, open the editor, generate persona feedback so inline notes appear.
2. Click a `.twyne-persona-note`, type a reply, ⌘↩: popover stays open, your reply appears
   as a bubble, a `typing…` indicator shows, then the persona's response appears inline.
   Send a second reply → multi-turn continues in place.
3. Open the Cast panel → the same thread is mirrored (sync intact).
4. Failure surfacing: signed out with no provider configured, replying shows the inline
   error instead of doing nothing.
5. Regression: clicking a note without replying still auto-closes on mouse-leave; "Strike"
   still dismisses; `npm run lint` + `bun test src/components/editor` pass.

## Out of scope
Streaming tokens for the reply (separate request), changing the Cast-panel thread UI, and
making non-persona (user) comments conversational.
